# Charter
In compliance with the newly approved City Ordinance No. 1, Series of 2025, which officially supersedes City Ordinance No. 7, Series of 2018, Gordon College affirms its full adherence to the updated directives as mandated by the local government. The institution remains committed to aligning its policies and operations with the latest regulatory framework to ensure a lawful, effective, and community-responsive higher educational institution.
[City-Ordinance-No.-01-s.-2025-An-Ordinance-Revising-the-Charter-of-Gordon-College](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/wp-content/uploads/2025/08/City-Ordinance-No.-01-s.-2025-An-Ordinance-Revising-the-Charter-of-Gordon-College.pdf)

# College Hymn
[![](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/play_button_gc-1.png)](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/gc-hymn.mp4)
The sea breeze over Subic Bay  
Moderate waves to mighty rest  
Sets our heart in inspiring pace  
How Gordon College made us best  
Raise our voices in silence  
Sing the praises in cadence  
Let these color our daily life  
And withstand the pilgrimage.  
Bless our Alma Mater  
Keep her firm and strong  
And be able to mold us all  
Set us upright on the road  
Hear the echo of Her song  
Feel the warmth of Her voice  
Keep the flame of the Torch  
At Gordon College proudly we belong.

# College Seal
This is the official college seal/logo of Gordon College. This is to be used in legal and formal documents and correspondence of the College. The colors green and yellow signify the community college’s commitment to sustainable development or a development for all generations as it works towards environmental sustainability in its attainment of its vision, mission and goals. “Ulo ng Apo” represents the city of Olongapo where the local Higher Education Institution is based. This symbolizes the ability of its students to lead and make a difference in the community.
The torch illuminates a book which represents the ability to serve as a guiding light for others. It also shows how education is a beacon of light that nurture learning. It is strategically placed beneath the Ulo ng Apo signifying that knowledge must be used, not for knowledge’s sake, but more for the community which includes its stakeholders.
The circular wreath made of interlocking branches and leaves represents the unity and strength of stakeholders and its students who stand among the distinguished scholars the local HEI molded and developed. With the wreath of laurel leaves being a symbol of honor and victory, students, faculty and the leaders of the institution are expected to achieve victory over ignorance while remaining honorable and preserving integrity towards excellence.
The platinum colored shield placed behind the Ulo ng Apo represents the boldness of its students in pursuit of new horizons of unparalleled intellectual success. To remind its students staff and all the members of the institution to be bold and brave enough to explore and seek new opportunities beyond the four corners of the college towards the development of the community they belong to.
Under the shield is the year that Gordon College was founded. This represents the number of years of upholding its values. Three values guide the college, namely: excellence; character and service which shall guide the institutions’ trifocal function of instruction, research and extension.
The logo is in a form of circle, symbolizing the perfect fulfillment of a holistic educational process.

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/about-us/history-of-gordon-college/
### Keywords: history, gc history, when founded, founded, established, background

# History of Gordon College
Gordon College was an off-shoot of the Olongapo City Training Center, then a training center for skilled manpower for employment at the former Naval facility of the United States. With the facility’s closure, and the slowing down of demand for skilled workers, City residents shifted to professional courses. The unavailability of affordable tertiary institutions for urban poor sector of the population led the City Government of Olongapo, during the incumbency of Hon. Katherine H. Gordon, then City Mayor, to open the Olongapo City Colleges through City Ordinance no. 9 series of 1999.
Initially, the College offered Bachelor of Science in Accountancy and Bachelor of Science in Computer Studies to 177 enrollees. Additional four-year programs were offered in the succeeding years to accommodate the demand of City residents for other programs.
City Ordinance no. 42 series of 2002 was passed in May 2002, providing a change in the name of Olongapo City Colleges to Gordon College, its present name, in honor of the Gordon family who greatly contributed to the development of the City of Olongapo. In the same year, the College was officially accepted as a member of the Association of Local Colleges and Universities (ALCU).
In 2004, a revision on the legal basis for the creation and operation of Gordon College was made through City Ordinance no. 36 series of 2004. In the same year, the College was granted by the Commission on Higher Education (CHED) Region III the certificate to operate with autonomy as per provisions of the Local Government Code of 19991. In addition to its undergraduate programs, the College opened its graduate programs in Education, Public Management and Business Administration.
In 2018, City Ordinance No. 07 series of 2018 was passed revising the charter of Gordon College subject to all Legal and Existing Rules and Regulations. This was superseded by City Ordinance No. 01 series of 2025 which was passed in 2025 that equipped Gordon College to expand its horizon for excellence to national and international level.

# Mandate and Function
Gordon College, formerly Olongapo City Colleges, is a local higher education institution established in 1999 under the City Government of Olongapo.
The College operates by virtue of City Ordinance No. 36, Series of 2004 and its amendment City Ordinance No. 1, Series of 2025 also known as the “ 2025 Revised Charter of Gordon College” that supersedes the City Ordinance No. 7, Series of 2018 . The proposed revision of the Charter of Gordon College aims to strengthen the promotion of excellence and delivery of education in the national and international level.
The Local Government of Olongapo supports and maintains its commitment to provide accessible and equitable distribution of opportunities to its constituents to acquire higher education, as well as, a responsive and relevant higher education institution.
### Legal Bases
* **CHED Memorandum Order No. 32, Series of 2006** – Policies, Standards and Guidelines on the Establishment and Operation of Local Colleges and Universities (LCUs);
* **CHED Memorandum Order No. 4, Series of 2007** – Implementing Rules of CM032, Series of 2006;
* **CHED Memorandum Order No. 42, Series of 2016** – Amendment of Article V, Section 5 and 12 of CMO 32, Series of 2006;
* **DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2009-67** – Guidelines on the Establishment of Local Colleges and Universities by Local Governments, and on the Operation of Higher Education Programs;
* **Republic Act No. 7722** – Higher Education Act of 1994;
* **Republic Act No. 9160** – Local Government code of 1991;
* **City Ordinance No. 1, Series of 2025** – 2025 Revised Charter of Gordon College
* **City Ordinance No. 7, Series of 2018** – Revised Charter of Gordon College of 2018;
* **City Ordinance No. 8, Series of 2010** – An Ordinance Amending the Legal Basis on the Creation and Operation of Gordon College;
* **Resolution No. 42, Series of 2002**– A Resolution Chaning the Name of Olongapo City Colleges to “Gordon College”;
* **City Ordinance No. 9, Series of 1999** – An Ordinance Creating Olongapo City Colleges

# Vision, Mission, Objectives, and Core Values
## Vision
A globally recognized local institution committed to innovative academic excellence, holistic and sustainable development, inclusivity, and community engagement.
## Mission
Produce empowered global citizens who create sustainable impact, uphold values of character, excellence, and service, and contribute to academic and societal development
## Strategic Objectives
1. **H**arness partnerships and linkages to produce graduates with global competencies aligned with the needs of the community and industry.
2. **O**rganize extension programs to sustain the greater impact of the institution to the community and environment.
3. **L**everage research outputs to improve existing knowledge and practices.
4. **I**ntegrate ethical principles and standards in the curriculum to strengthen the moral responsibilities of lifelong learners.
5. **S**upport continuous professional development and well-being of employees.
6. **T**ailor-fit student support services to ensure students thrive in the rapidly changing world.
7. **I**nvest in state-of-the-art facilities and technologies to enhance teaching, learning and research.
8. **C**ultivate a culture that embraces diversity and inclusivity.
## Core Values
* **Character.** Character is the foundation of a meaningful life. The institution strives to instill in students a strong moral compass, integrity, and a sense of responsibility for their actions. This includes fostering critical thinking skills, respect for diversity, and a commitment to a lifelong learning.
* **Excellence.** The institution provides a rigorous academic environment that challenges students to reach their full potential. Intellectual curiosity, innovation, and a pursuit of knowledge for its won sake is encouraged. It is believed that excellence is not just about achieving high grades, but also about developing the ability to analyze complex problems, think creatively, and communicate effectively.
* **Service.** Education empowers individuals to make a positive impact on their communities and the world. Students are encouraged to engage in service-learning opportunities that allow them to apply their knowledge and skills to real-world problems. The institution fosters a culture of social responsibility where students develop a sense of compassion and a desire to contribute to the betterment of society.

# Colleges
**Institute of Graduate Studies**
The mission of the Institute of Graduate Studies of Gordon College is to provide quality master’s degree programs and opportunities for continuing professional and career development for students whose academic preparation and personal characteristics foresee success in graduate studies. Each graduate program is firmly grounded in theoretical and empirical bodies of knowledge. Students are challenged to think critically and creatively, and to apply theories to practice in an atmosphere of inquiry and dynamic exchange with faculty and each other
**College of Allied Health Studies**
By 2025, the College of Allied Health Studies is envisioned to be a Center of Excellence in Allied Health Education in Region III and dedicated to being a forerunner in providing research-inclined instruction, evidence-based practice and community–responsive health care education committed to the holistic development of the human person.
**College of Business and Accountancy**
A center of excellence in accounting and business education that develops competitive leaders and professionals for providing professional training, innovative research and relevant extension services.
**College of Computer Studies**
The College of Computer Studies shall be the Center of Excellence in Information Technology and Computer Science for providing professional and technological training, scientific and innovative, and relevant community service
**College of Education, Arts and Sciences**
We envision the Gordon College-College of Education, Arts and Sciences to be a center of excellence in the field for providing professional training, innovative research and community service.
**College of Hospitality and Tourism Management**
To be a center of excellence in tourism and hospitality management with high standard through instruction, research and extension.

# Academic Affairs
Academic affairs is the institutional office that oversees a variety of academic programs and departments at a school. The head of this office supports curriculum initiatives, faculty hiring and promotion events, faculty research and teaching, and all academic departments and programs.
## Offices Under the Academic Affair’s Office
* Institute of Graduate Studies
* College of Allied Health Studies
* College of Business and Accountancy
* College of Computer Studies
* College of Education, Arts, and Sciences
* College of Hospitality and Tourism Management
* National Service Training Program
* Open and Distance Learning/ Vocational and Technical Education
* Cosme Y. Sering Library (College Library)
* Office of Instruction
* Educational Resources Development Unit

# Administration and Finance
The department is in charge of providing efficient, economical, and effective services relating to personnel, supplies, equipment, collection, disbursement, security and custody of property.

# Institutional Planning, Development, and External Affairs (IPDEA)
## Vision
An innovation hub for strategic development and global relations that fosters cross-cultural understanding and builds local and international linkages for academic excellence.
## Mission
Lead strategic planning and global engagement in Gordon College by facilitating inclusive collaboration and international partnerships to achieve academic excellence and empower global citizens.
## Offices Under IPDEA
• Quality Assurance and Accreditation  
• International Affairs Unit  
• Partnerships and Linkages Unit  
• Diversity, Equity and Inclusion Unit  
• Alumni Affairs Unit

# Office of the College President
This office exercises overall responsibility and supervision over the College. The Chief Executive Officer of the College which is the College President is responsible for the articulation of the vision, mission, goals and objectives of the College and the development and implementation of educational programs and policies to achieve them. The office is directly accountable to the Board of Trustees for the organization, administration and supervision of the College.
## Offices Under the Office of the College President (OCP)
* Executive Council
* Management Council
* Information and Communication Services Unit
* Office of the College Registrar
* UniFAST Unit and Scholarship Affairs Unit

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/admission/continuing-students-returnees-and-cross-enrollment/
### Keywords: continuing students, returnees, cross enrollment, cross-enroll

# Continuing Students, Returnees and Cross Enrollment
## Continuing Students and Returnees
Continuing students are those who were previously enrolled in the College but unable to enroll for at least one semester and decided to continue studying in GC. These students have not requested for any transfer credential. They may have or have not filed for Leave of Absence.
Returning students are those who requested for transfer credentials but decided to re-enroll in the College. Returning students shall no longer be required to take the admission test.
## Cross Enrollment
* **GC Students to Enroll in Other Schools**
  + Students shall not be allowed to cross-enroll without proper consultation and consent from their respective College Deans. Students shall not be allowed to cross-enroll if the subject/course is not open during his/her last two semesters in the College. He/She shall request recommendation from his/her Dean and authorization coming from the College Registrar before he/she can cross-enroll to other colleges/universities.
* **Students from Other Schools to Cross-enroll in GC**
  + Students from other schools who would cross-enroll in GC should present a written permission from his school that he/she is allowed to enroll in GC.
- ## Getting Admitted
- * [Continuing Students, Returnees and Cross Enrollment](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/admission/continuing-students-returnees-and-cross-enrollment/)

### Keywords: freshmen, new students, first year, requirements, gcat

# Freshmen Students
## Admission Requirements
Admission to Gordon College shall be open to Filipino citizens, both residents and non-residents of Olongapo City, Non-Filipino citizens, high school graduates, or passers of the high school equivalency certificate (PEPT), and shows evidence of being able to benefit from instruction. A NON-RESIDENT is defined as any individual who has lived less than one year in Olongapo City, is not registered to vote in Olongapo City and/or does not pay property taxes or contributes to the economic development of Olongapo City.
## New College Freshmen Students for Undergraduate Programs
* Admission to the chosen degree program will be based on an applicant’s Gordon College Admission Test (GCAT) results and previous high school/college academic records.
* Original copy of High School Report Card (F138/SF9) or Original ALS Certificate of Rating Secondary Level for ALS Passers
* Certificate of Good Moral Character
* Copy of Birth Certificate
* Medical Certificate (Issued by the Gordon College Clinic)
* Barangay Certificate of Residency
* One 2×2 picture
- ## Getting Admitted
- * [Continuing Students, Returnees and Cross Enrollment](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/admission/continuing-students-returnees-and-cross-enrollment/)
  * [Freshmen Students](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/admission/freshmen-students/)

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/admission/igs-institute-of-graduate-studies-students/
### Keywords: igs, graduate studies, master, graduate admission

# IGS (Institute of Graduate Studies) Students
To qualify for admission to the Institute of Graduate Studies, an applicant must meet the following general and specific standards:
1. Student must have a minimum undergraduate GPA of 2.00 or 85% (except NSTP and PE). Student with lower general average than the prescribed rating may be admitted to the master’s program on probation. While on probationary status, s/he is allowed to carry the maximum load of only six (6) units per term. S/he will be placed in full standing status after having obtained an average rating of 1.5 or better in the first 12 units.
2. Specific programs may have different requirement as well as additional requirements such as materials and a personal interview, to wit:
   1. Master in Public Administration
      * At least two (2) years of government/ public service in any capacity
   2. Master in Business Management
      * BS Commerce or BS Business Administration graduate
      * Non-business course graduate must finish 18 tool courses. While earning these units, s/he may enroll any two basic/major subjects in the MBM program.
      * At least two (2) years of relevant experience/ service.
   3. Master of Arts in Education
      * Should be an education graduate
      * For non-education graduate, at least 24 units of professional education in the undergraduate level.
      * At least two (2) years of teaching experience/ service.
   4. Master of Arts in Nursing
      * Should be a Registered Nurse
      * At least two (2) years of experience in nursing and/or related field.
3. Pass the qualifying examination and interview.
4. All applicants for admission must submit the following;
   * Original Transcript of Records from recognized colleges or universities (with remarks, “Copy for Gordon College” or “Granted Honorable Dismissal”).
   * Two ID pictures (2×2/passport size)
   * Certificate of Employment
   * A Php200.00 non-refundable fee for application processing.
   * Additional materials, if required by specific program.

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/campus-life/student-publication/
### Keywords: student publication, the forefront, campus paper, student newspaper

# Student Publication
**The Forefront** is the official student publication of Gordon College located in Olongapo City Sports Complex, Donor Street, East Tapinac, Olongapo City 2200. Its office is located at Rm. 516, 5th Floor, GC Main Building.
The Forefront was reestablished on June 2009, after a four-year hiatus, with the desire to promote the development and growth of responsible journalism as a means of strengthening ethical values, encouraging critical and creative thinking, and developing moral character and personal discipline in accordance with the Campus Journalism Act of 1991 (R.A. 7079).
**THE LOGO**
The official logo of the Forefront, as appeared above, is consist of two elements and two colors.
A *quill or pluma* refers to the writing profession. Its color, red, symbolizes courage and sacrifice. A *stem of laurel leaves* signifies triumph. Its color, golden yellow, symbolizes the nobility of the writing profession. The quill and the stem of laurel leaves form a capital letter “F” which stands for The Forefront.
**General Principles and Objectives**
The Forefront lives with its tagline, **“Informing and Empowering.”** To be able to uphold with its battlecry for campus press freedom, the Editorial Board sets the following General Principles:
1. The Forefront shall serve primarily as a newspaper of and for the students. It shall protect and promote the constitutionally mandated rights of students to free speech and of expression. In addition, it shall protect and promote the students’ rights and welfare.
2. The Forefront, being a publication of the students devoted to the ideals of truth, shall pursue the mission of social transformation through active and responsible journalism.
3. The Forefront, being an independent institution, shall not be subjected to any form of censorship. In turn, the editors and staff shall see to it that the Forefront exercises academic and campus press freedom in accordance with the laws of the Republic of the Philippines. It shall also seek to protect and promote  
   press freedom by taking firm and assertive stance against elements endangering such right.
4. The editors shall respect the rights of other parties that are equally protected by the laws of the Republic of the Philippines, affording them the right and opportunity to refute or reply to criticism or comment hurled at them.
Along with this principles, The Forefront shall at all times strive to:
1. Inform the public (i.e. the general population of the College, including the administration and the general public outside the College) with news of relevance and interest;
2. Serve as a venue for campus opinion and in maintaining an atmosphere of free and responsible discussion and of intellectual exploration;
3. Provide an outlet for students’ suggestions for the betterment of the campus;
4. Develop students’ powers of observation and discrimination concerning relative merits of news articles and;
5. Provide an opportunity for journalistic and creative writing.
[](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/366944890_1400491360869874_3388834360043358186_n.mp4)

# A Testament to Quality Education: GC Honors NLE Top 9 Topnotcher Alexis Molon Penuliar
[Image: A Testament to Quality Education: GC Honors NLE Top 9 Topnotcher Alexis Molon Penuliar]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, April 14, 2026**
TOP 9 | Congratulations to our NLE Topnotcher, Alexis Molon Penuliar!
On April 12, 2026, at the SMX Convention Center in Pasay City, Gordon College proudly stood in honor at the Oathtaking Ceremony of newly registered nurses, commemorating a significant milestone that marks the beginning of a noble journey in healthcare.
Being recognized as one of the top-performing institutions reflects our unwavering commitment to quality education and our mission to develop competent and compassionate nurses.
This achievement is a testament to the unwavering support and visionary leadership of Rolen C. Paulino Jr.; Cristabelle Marie C. Paulino; Dr. Imelda D.P. Soriano; and our dedicated academic leaders: Dr. Darwin P. Paguio (VPAA), Dr. Victor C. Quimen Jr., Ms. Sheila Marie P. Oconer, and Ms. April Joyce Liban-Dela Cruz.
A heartfelt gratitude is extended to our BSN faculty, city and college officials, personnel, and all stakeholders who continue to champion the [#TatakGC](https://www.facebook.com/hashtag/tatakgc?__cft__[0]=AZYa4p0IZUIBqOY-hlFJ0u-pUZCuy_GKOKicmf7ktFWNDgsSLMQQu0oI5s_r4byAVdZJf34KwdSH8GOlMSGW_WFBwDHZ_nPRvaLh13fmU6Dp5Chxp6N0eQHveC7Vd2ZsYl16qaXziM1PDxQf-V_RD6h_&__tn__=*NK-R) brand of excellence.
[#TatakGC](https://www.facebook.com/hashtag/tatakgc?__cft__[0]=AZYa4p0IZUIBqOY-hlFJ0u-pUZCuy_GKOKicmf7ktFWNDgsSLMQQu0oI5s_r4byAVdZJf34KwdSH8GOlMSGW_WFBwDHZ_nPRvaLh13fmU6Dp5Chxp6N0eQHveC7Vd2ZsYl16qaXziM1PDxQf-V_RD6h_&__tn__=*NK-R)

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/%f0%9d%90%83%f0%9d%90%ab-%f0%9d%90%8f%f0%9d%90%9a%f0%9d%90%a0%f0%9d%90%ae%f0%9d%90%a2%f0%9d%90%a8-%f0%9d%90%a8%f0%9d%90%a7-%f0%9d%90%86%f0%9d%90%82%f0%9d%90%ac-%f0%9d%90%8c%f0%9d%90%9a

# 𝐃𝐫. 𝐏𝐚𝐠𝐮𝐢𝐨 𝐨𝐧 𝐆𝐂’𝐬 𝐌𝐚𝐜 𝐋𝐚𝐛: 𝟏𝐬𝐭 𝐢𝐧 𝐙𝐚𝐦𝐛𝐚𝐥𝐞𝐬, 𝟏𝐬𝐭 𝐢𝐧 𝐑𝐞𝐠𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐈𝐈𝐈
[Image: 𝐃𝐫. 𝐏𝐚𝐠𝐮𝐢𝐨 𝐨𝐧 𝐆𝐂’𝐬 𝐌𝐚𝐜 𝐋𝐚𝐛: 𝟏𝐬𝐭 𝐢𝐧 𝐙𝐚𝐦𝐛𝐚𝐥𝐞𝐬, 𝟏𝐬𝐭 𝐢𝐧 𝐑𝐞𝐠𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐈𝐈𝐈]
[Campus](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/campus/) [CCS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ccs/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, May 22, 2025**
Gordon College (GC), through the College of Computer Studies (CCS), opened its Mac Laboratory on the 5th floor of the building, room 507, last April 28.
This new facility aims to equip students with the necessary competencies to keep up with the quick growth of technological advancements.
According to Dr. Imelda D.P. Soriano, the College President, the Mac Laboratory aims to innovate the school through integrating smart classrooms over the years and to, ideally, attract international students, preparing the students for the evolution of Gordon College into a university.
Meanwhile, in a previous report by The Forefront, students from CCS initiated the GC CCS Tech Fest as a fundraising event last February for the improvements of the Mac Lab room and some of its equipment.
When interviewed again, Kurt Jeehan Terado, one of the event’s heads, expressed his joy because their collective effort as students manifested in their school.
“Knowing that we were able to contribute something that future students will benefit from, I feel like everyone from our batch felt fulfilled,” said Terado.
To add to their efforts, the Mac Lab units came from the Local Government Unit (LGU) as the funds raised through this event are for the sole-purpose of room improvements.
Moreover, Dr. Darwin Paguio, Vice President of Academic Affairs, declared his pride for GC’s achievement.
“The first local college na magkaka-Mac laboratory. [The] first in zambales, [the] first in region 3,” Dr. Paguio stated.
With GC’s climb towards innovation, the institution also eyes international deployment of its students in Macau.
[Image: ✍🏻]
: John Carlo Bermudez | Associate Editor

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/the-celebration-of-arts-and-culture-ignites-a-fiery-pase-community-concert-this-27th-founding-anniversary

# The celebration of arts and culture ignites a fiery PASE Community Concert this 27th Founding Anniversary
[Image: The celebration of arts and culture ignites a fiery PASE Community Concert this 27th Founding Anniversary]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, February 25, 2026**
The Performing Arts Student Ensemble (PASE) ignites a synergy of performances on the second day of the 27th Founding Anniversary on February 25, at East Tapinac Oval Grounds.
The first segment featured PASE Ballo’s contemporary dance performance to the song ‘Tala,’ touching the hearts of GCians with their moving steps.
Furthermore, PASE Voci’s chorale performance filled the air with harmonic voices and unity, creating an ethereal atmosphere through their cover of ‘Bagong Umaga’ by Bayang Barrios.
The third segment was a play by PASE Teatro, showcasing relatable acting that highlighted the reality of social media’s impact on people’s lives.
In addition, PASE Ballo returned for another performance of a cultural inspired dance filled with enthusiasm and energy.
Moreover, the fifth segment was a collaboration between all PASE ensembles — Cuerdas, Teatro, and Voci. Storytelling and music united on a narrative of Overseas Filipino Workers (OFWs) acted by Teatro and set to Voci and Cuerdas’s rendition of ‘Pinoy Ako’ by Bamboo.
Following that is a performance from Teatro’s newest troupe, the collectives. The four-member rap group sang Filipino hip-hop songs that captured the culture of Pinoy music history. They performed covers of ‘Venus’ by Ron Henely, ‘Ikaw lang’ by Kiyo, and ‘Upuan’ by Gloc 9, bringing back nostalgic vibes to many GCians.
Finally, the last segment featured a collaboration of Voci and Cuerdas. Following this is PASE Ballo’s dance performance, celebrating Olongapo’s culture and the jeepney tradition. The performance transitioned to Cuerdas and Voci, who performed a reggae song.
The event proudly celebrated National Arts Month this February, showcasing the diverse talents and artistry of GCians. Through various performances, the students highlighted their creativity and passion for the arts, demonstrating the vibrant cultural landscape of the institution. It was a testament to the rich artistic expression and talent that GCians have, making it a memorable celebration of arts and culture, leaving a lasting impression on the audiences at the 27th founding anniversary. | via Lei Ydah Lasala Ledesma / The FOREFRONT
Photos by Franz Feria & Lauren Louise Sanita

# 𝐌𝐀𝐘𝐀𝐑𝐈 𝐑𝐢𝐬𝐞𝐬: 𝐆𝐂 𝐥𝐚𝐮𝐧𝐜𝐡𝐞𝐬 𝐟𝐢𝐫𝐬𝐭 𝐋𝐆𝐁𝐓𝐐𝐈𝐀+ 𝐒𝐭𝐮𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐎𝐫𝐠𝐚𝐧𝐢𝐳𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧
[Image: 𝐌𝐀𝐘𝐀𝐑𝐈 𝐑𝐢𝐬𝐞𝐬: 𝐆𝐂 𝐥𝐚𝐮𝐧𝐜𝐡𝐞𝐬 𝐟𝐢𝐫𝐬𝐭 𝐋𝐆𝐁𝐓𝐐𝐈𝐀+ 𝐒𝐭𝐮𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐎𝐫𝐠𝐚𝐧𝐢𝐳𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, June 18, 2025**
Gordon College established its first LGBTQIA+ Student Organization named “Mga Anak ng Yugtong Apo para sa Respeto at Inklusyon (MAYARI),” in line with the celebration of Pride Month this June.
MAYARI aims to champion the rights and well-being of LGBTQIA+ students within the college and beyond.
Furthermore, Mr. Marko Aratea, organization adviser of MAYARI, confirmed that the organization has developed rapidly and has profound objectives.
“We, at MAYARI, are currently in the process of becoming a formally recognized civic organization at Gordon College, but we will get there very soon!” Mr. Aratea stated.
Moreover, MAYARI was formed with both students and the Office of Student Welfare and Services (OSWS) in mind as a dedicated community for LGBTQIA+ students and their allies. Both Mr. Aratea and co-adviser Mr. Christian Loid Valenzuela started as the founding members who led the creation of the constitution, set clear objectives, and met institutional standards.
The central aim of MAYARI is to establish a safe and inclusive space for LGBTQIA+ students. Mr. Aratea outlined the organization’s primary objectives: “MAYARI aims to provide a safe and inclusive space for LGBTQIA+ students and allies in our humble institution. Our main goals are to promote visibility, inclusivity, respect, empowerment, and solidarity.”
MAYARI intends to accomplish these objectives through education by providing LGBTQIA+ seminars, webinars, gender sensitivity programs, and opportunities for creativity and leadership.
Furthermore, one of the important goals of MAYARI is to handle issues affecting LGBTQIA+ individuals, mainly discrimination, mental health challenges, and lack of community understanding.
“MAYARI is a civic organization for LGBTQIA+ and allies in GC, also serving as a platform to address catalysts related to gender-based concerns through formal dialogues programs and activities that will equip relevant stakeholders regarding said concerns and practical solutions,” MAYARI’s adviser affirmed.
Meanwhile, the organization will implement peer support initiatives, explore partnerships with the college’s Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) office, and launch extensive awareness campaigns on SOGIESC (Sexual Orientation, Gender Identity, Gender Expression, and Sex Characteristics).
Looking beyond symbolic gestures, MAYARI envisions a future where “total acceptance” is deeply ingrained in the institution’s policies and culture.
“Total acceptance means more than symbolic gestures—it involves policy and culture change,” Mr. Aratea emphasized and further discussed that the organization aims to integrate inclusivity into the college’s systemic operations rather than limiting it to mere statements.

# Gender and Development and Mental Health Awareness Seminar
[Image: Gender and Development and Mental Health Awareness Seminar]
[Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/extension/) [GAD](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/gad/) [RDCES](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/rdces/) [Community Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/community-extension/)
**Thursday, May 25, 2023**
The GC CESU, in partnership with the GC GAD Unit and the GC Guidance Office, successfully organized a comprehensive seminar. The event consisted of morning and afternoon sessions and actively involved grade 12 senior high school students from Tapinac Senior High School. Held at the GC Function Hall, the seminar provided an ideal platform for the exchange of valuable insights and knowledge on these important subjects. The presence of esteemed resource speakers, Ms. Abigail Legaspi (GAD Unit Head), Ms. Mechel Bautista (GC Guidance Office Head), and Ms. JaimJaime Nhilen Apostol (GC Guidance Designate), contributed to the seminar’s excellent delivery.

# GC Welcomes Konyang University of South Korea
[Image: GC Welcomes Konyang University of South Korea]
[IPDEA](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ipdea/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, May 14, 2025**
On May 14, 2025, Gordon College warmly welcomed delegates from Konyang University, South Korea, as part of an initiative to explore academic collaboration between the two institutions. The delegation was composed of Ms. Youngmi Sohn, Head of the Global Education Innovation Headquarters, and Mr. Dong-Yeon Ko, Team Leader.
The visit aimed to strengthen international ties, particularly in the field of Allied Health Studies, and included a tour of the college’s facilities—classrooms, laboratories, and library—especially in relation to Konyang’s interest in developing a short-term language program.
During the meeting, the delegates expressed their interest in discussing various opportunities for collaboration and confirmed that they are currently reviewing the proposed Memorandum of Agreement (MOA). Both institutions expressed optimism for a sustainable and meaningful partnership that supports global academic engagement and student development.
[#SDG4QualityEducation](https://www.facebook.com/hashtag/sdg4qualityeducation?__eep__=6&__cft__[0]=AZU-MJjDycGDH_UaQtwT_4J-j316VVel5iP5ryrRoq_9UE5HJ5ob8YMUzXXskfRuXJS4_SeA1HBK-G13k0RcdchS3QnQjHJaS2G2cb_C6G7hoUHHTzT1PvWwYoEvycwasw2gkCy7qyjUFMbHlrBIsJrxcrBMZozIhMNwMF50pfMefjqdk2kIQ0MoKPuBEFOdlNY4GORJhq_mo0Wcr5b_FRU_PsrhZbGQmwjntb0Bp9RSIQ&__tn__=*NK-y-R)[#SDG17PartnershipsForTheGoals](https://www.facebook.com/hashtag/sdg17partnershipsforthegoals?__eep__=6&__cft__[0]=AZU-MJjDycGDH_UaQtwT_4J-j316VVel5iP5ryrRoq_9UE5HJ5ob8YMUzXXskfRuXJS4_SeA1HBK-G13k0RcdchS3QnQjHJaS2G2cb_C6G7hoUHHTzT1PvWwYoEvycwasw2gkCy7qyjUFMbHlrBIsJrxcrBMZozIhMNwMF50pfMefjqdk2kIQ0MoKPuBEFOdlNY4GORJhq_mo0Wcr5b_FRU_PsrhZbGQmwjntb0Bp9RSIQ&__tn__=*NK-y-R)

# CEAS Mentors Duo Reclaims Gold, Overpowers CBA in Mixed Doubles Badminton Clash
[Image: CEAS Mentors Duo Reclaims Gold, Overpowers CBA in Mixed Doubles Badminton Clash]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, September 17, 2025**
TRICKSHOT COVERED COURT—In a smashing display of speed, precision, and teamwork, the College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS) Mentors reclaimed badminton glory, dominating the mixed doubles championship against the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) Wizards on September 16, 2025, at the Trickshot Badminton Court.
Right from the opening serve, the dynamic duo of Daniel Ruzzel Bernal and Mary Anne Lafuente wasted no time asserting their dominance with lightning-quick reflexes and razor-sharp shot placements.
Consequently, their rapid-fire rallies and clever net play kept the CBA Wizards scrambling across the court, ending the first set in convincing fashion at 21-9, with CEAS clearly in control.
Building on that first-set momentum, Bernal and Lafuente came out swinging in the second set, smashing their way to an early lead through seamless communication and smart positioning.
Meanwhile, although the Wizards attempted a comeback with aggressive clears and pushes, every move was countered by the Mentors’ calculated drop shots, steady defense, and powerful smashes, closing the set at 21-1 to secure the championship crown.
After the final rally, Lafuente emphasized that the victory was not only a triumph of skill but also a testament to their trust in each other, both on and off the court.
Similarly, Bernal expressed his pride and excitement after clinching the gold, saying, “Sobrang saya po and super proud po ako sa partner ko.”
Before reaching the finals, the duo battled past the CHTM Sharks in the elimination round, overcoming sharp smashes and relentless returns to secure a 30–10 win and a spot in the championship stage.
Furthermore, the CEAS pair showed that age and experience were no barriers, as their first match as college students turned into a golden opportunity to prove their fire and determination. | via Lovely Mae M. Doroin/The FOREFRONTFOREFRONT

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/from-debits-to-dominance-pink-team-cpinka-credits-the-ultimate-gamble-victory

# From Debits to Dominance: Pink Team (cPINKa) Credits the Ultimate Gamble Victory
[Image: From Debits to Dominance: Pink Team (cPINKa) Credits the Ultimate Gamble Victory]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, March 1, 2026**
Accountancy students of Gordon College gathered at East Tapinac Covered Court on February 25 for Junior Philippine Institute of Accountants (JPIA)’s Ultimate Gamble Team Building.
The participants collaborated, competed, and conquered through strategy-driven games that tested teamwork and unity.
Demy Anne M. Roallos headed the Green Team (Berde Makausad), Triskelly Lianne Cyril Cabildo led the Red Team (Pulang Ina), Felicity Dyle Rivas guided the Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca), Aran Benz C. Sardañas captained the Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada), Shakira Nicole B. Guintu steered the Pink Team (cPINKa – Certified Pink Accountants), Jan Lester M. Bitangcol led the Purple Team (Ooh-Lila), and Hannah Jhem M. Malit directed the Blue Team (BIR – Bluereau of Impeccable Recorders).
As the event began, each team performed their energetic yells and presented their flags to start the event with a blast. In the first game entitled “Caterpillar Game,” Green Team (Berde Makausad) won first place, Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada) gained second place, and Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca) took third place.
For the second game of “Pass the Balloon,” Red Team (Pulang Ina) achieved the first place, followed by the Pink Team (cPINKa), and lastly, the Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca). As the third game commenced, entitled “The Floor is Lava,” Pink Team (cPINKa) was hailed as the champion, Green Team (Berde Makausad) as the first runner-up, and the Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada) as the second runner-up.
Moreover, in the station games, the teams played in seven stations, namely Pass the Ball, Cut it Up, Wrap-tastic, Balloon Cup Relay, Rollercoaster Ball, T-Shirt Relay, and Calcu Relay. Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca) triumphed in first place, Purple Team (Ooh-Lila) succeeded in second place, while Pink Team (cPINKa) acquired third place.
After consolidating all performance entries like preparing a final financial statement, Pink Team (cPINKa) emerged as the overall champion with a total audited score of 185 points, followed by Green Team (Berde Makausad) with 180 points, Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca) with 175 points, Blue Team (BIR) and Red Team (Pulang Ina) who tied with 170 points, and lastly, Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada) and Purple Team (Ooh-Lila) who both garnered 150 points. Furthermore, Blue Team (BIR) won for the best yell, while Red Team (Pulang Ina) claimed the best flag award.
According to Pink (cPINKa) team leader, Shakira Nicole B. Guintu, their victory came from proper planning before the activities and going all-in on collaboration. “No’ng nag-activity po kami, laging may nagsasalita and nakikinig. Pwedeng higher batch, pwedeng lower batch, basta lahat naman po kami nakikipag-cooperate,” said Guintu.
Guintu also added that the team building reflected real-world challenges in accountancy, helping them to be patient and to remain calm under pressure. “May activities na parang paulit-ulit na kami pero nire-remind namin na kalmahan lang, na i-go lang sa flow namin, sa pace namin,” the team leader stated.
The activity ultimately reinforced that future accountants must not only master numbers but also embody the 3 C’s—Collaborate, Compete, and Conquer—skills essential in navigating real-world professional engagements and organizational challenges. | via Justine Stephanie A. Canlas/The FOREFRONT

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-presents-one-thousand-students-in-graduation-rites

# GC presents one thousand students in graduation rites
[Image: GC presents one thousand students in graduation rites]
[Campus](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/campus/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, July 10, 2025**
Gordon College’s (GC) 23rd Commencement Exercises saw a total of 1,016 passionate graduates walk in the aisle of Subic Bay Exhibition and Convention Center (SBECC) this year, donning their respective togas and sablays.
Amidst heavy rains, Cluster I filled the morning ceremony with 487 graduates from the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) and Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM).
Meanwhile, 529 students from Cluster II’s College of Allied Health Sciences (CAHS), College of Computer Studies (CCS), and College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) graced the stage during the afternoon rites.
The graduates are products of Free Higher Education (FHE) from the institution and taxpayers, who enjoyed it during their tenure.

# Fifty Titles Received from Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas
[Image: Fifty Titles Received from Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas]
[LIMC](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/limc/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, April 21, 2022**
The Gordon College Library and Instructional Media Center (LIMC) welcomed the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) in giving them fifty (50) titles of learning resources from their Knowledge Resource Network Collection.   
Mr. Marlon Atencio, College Librarian received the learning resources from the representatives of the BSP.

# SUSPENSION OF TRANSACTIONS (Finance Services Unit)
[Image: SUSPENSION OF TRANSACTIONS (Finance Services Unit)]
**Tuesday, July 8, 2025**
ANNOUNCEMENT
July 8, 2025
SUSPENSION OF TRANSACTIONS
In line with the upcoming Gordon College 23rd Commencement Exercises on July 9, 2025, all transactions of the Finance Management Services Unit will be suspended.
Services will resume on Thursday, July 10, 2025.

# Hindi pula’t dilaw ang tunay na magkalaban
[Image: Hindi pula’t dilaw ang tunay na magkalaban]
[CAHS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/cahs/) [CBA](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/cba/) [CCS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ccs/) [CEAS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ceas/) [CHTM](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/chtm/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, September 7, 2025**
Gordon College, OLONGAPO CITY—Ibinandera nina Lourd Cedric Alejo ng College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) at Pauline Batinga ng College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) ang kani-kanilang kolehiyo matapos koronahan bilang bagong Lakan at Lakambini ng Buwan ng Wika 2025 noong ika-4 ng Sityembre.
Bukod sa prestihiyosong korona, itinanghal ding Lakan ng Larawan 2025 si Alejo habang nasungkit naman ni Rowena Ablian mula sa College of Allied Health Studies ang titulo ng Lakambini ng Larawan 2025.
Kinilala namang Lakan at Lakambini ng Pormal na Kasuotan sina Matthew Navarro (CHTM) at Shanelle Ferrer ng College of Computer Studies (CCS).
Batay sa kanilang ranggo, hinirang ang mga sumusunod:
Lakan at Lakambini ng Wika (1st Runner-up)
Matthew Navarro (CHTM) at Rowena Ablian (CAHS)
Lakan at Lakambini ng Panitikan (2nd Runner-up)
Henry Agustin Baja Jr. (CAHS) at Shanelle Ferrer (CCS)
Lakan at Lakambini ng Sining (3rd Runner-up)
Reyelle Ariza (CEAS) at Angel Mae Deseo (CEAS)
Lakan at Lakambini ng SamFil (4th Runner-up)
Clark Justin Pambid (CCS) at Luisa May Manlapat (CBA)
Bilang bahagi ng taunang pagdiriwang ng Buwan ng Wika, ang naturang patimpalak ay isinagawa sa pangunguna ng Samahang Filipino (SamFil). | via Keane Rafael Abdon/The FOREFRONT

# Gordon College Faculty Join Basic FSL Training to Promote Inclusive Education
[Image: Gordon College Faculty Join Basic FSL Training to Promote Inclusive Education]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, August 29, 2025**
Faculty members from the College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS) and the College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) with Mr. Joseph Atencio-OIC, Head of Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) Unit proudly took part in a three-day Basic Filipino Sign Language (FSL) Training held last August 26–28, 2025 at the SMX Convention Center, Olongapo City. The event was organized by the Olongapo City Persons with Disability Affairs Office (PDAO) in collaboration with the Department of Education – Olongapo City Division Office.
Throughout the training, participants engaged in interactive and hands-on activities designed to equip educators with the skills to communicate effectively with the Deaf—nurturing a more inclusive, and compassionate learning environment.
This initiative not only strengthened our instructors’ professional competencies but also reaffirmed their dedication to accessibility, diversity, and inclusivity in education—a true reflection of Gordon College’s commitment to ensuring that no learner is left behind.

# Gordon College Joins the 2025 Times Higher Education Impact Rankings
[Image: Gordon College Joins the 2025 Times Higher Education Impact Rankings]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, June 18, 2025**
We are proud to announce that Gordon College has officially earned a place in the prestigious Times Higher Education (THE) Impact Rankings 2025! Despite being a newcomer to the global stage, GC was ranked among the top 1,500+ out of 2,526 higher education institutions (HEIs) from 130 countries, assessed for their contributions to achieving the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (UN SDGs)—a recognition based on universities’ social impact through research, stewardship, outreach, and teaching.
Gordon College participated in four SDG categories and earned the following standings:
• SDG 3 – Good Health and Well-being: Ranked 1001–1500
• SDG 4 – Quality Education: Ranked 1501+
• SDG 10 – Reduced Inequalities: Ranked 1001+
• SDG 17 – Partnerships for the Goals: Ranked 1501+
More than just recognition, this milestone reflects our evolving vision—to become a globally recognized local institution making meaningful contributions to global progress through inclusive, equitable, and sustainable development in higher education.
[Image: 🔗]
View the full rankings here: [https://www.timeshighereducation.com/impactrankings](https://www.timeshighereducation.com/impactrankings?fbclid=IwZXh0bgNhZW0CMTAAYnJpZBExcTNkc2RsSjBLTDYxc2tHYwEejGKbxvzhMrtl29ZxRx90Hnq6JINWNlRYH9NAwdKPUl7niNP38O8MykS9cpg_aem_FulevGowcrEUlih87Sa6UQ)

# Gordon College proudly welcomes the CHEDRO III Technical Panel for Nursing
[Image: Gordon College proudly welcomes the CHEDRO III Technical Panel for Nursing]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, June 4, 2025**
Gordon College proudly welcomes the CHEDRO III Technical Panel for Nursing, the Technical Evaluator for Nursing, and the Regional Quality Assessment Team (RQAT) for the evaluation of our Master of Arts in Nursing (MAN) Program on May 29–30, 2025.
This collaborative engagement forms part of Gordon College’s initiative to apply for the Certificate of Program Compliance (COPC) and reflects our commitment to quality assurance, academic excellence, and continuous improvement in graduate nursing education.

# Category 2 Offenses
[Image: Category 2 Offenses]
[OSWS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/osws/)
**Thursday, August 17, 2023**
***Eyes here GCians!***
To ensure your harmonious stay in the campus and a safe space for every GCian, it is imperative that you are aware of the **Student Code of Discipline**.
Below are the offenses under **Category 2**.
In the event you have committed a violation, here is the **Intervention and Disciplinary Procedure for Category 2:**  
1st Offense – suspesion (3 to 5 days)  
2nd Offense – minimum of 7 days to 1 semester (as may be determined by the discipline committee)  
3rd Offense – dismissal/ expulsion/ exclusion may be determined by the discipline committee

# CHTM Sharks Roar at 2nd CHTM Rally 2025
[Image: CHTM Sharks Roar at 2nd CHTM Rally 2025]
[CHTM](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/chtm/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, September 11, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE — The College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) filled the East Tapinac Covered Court with cheers and chants as the 2nd CHTM Rally unfolded last September 9, 2025, igniting the spirit of the CHTM Sharks in preparation for the much-anticipated Siklab 2025 Sportsfest on September 15–19, 2025.
The event showcased the final lineup of the College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) official teams and players.
Furthermore, athletes from the CHTM Sharks introduced themselves through lively intermission numbers performed by each sports team, adding excitement and entertainment to the rally.
Player representatives from each team also participated in a brief interview with the Masters of Ceremony, where they shared their thoughts and enthusiasm for the upcoming Siklab competition.
Moreover, the event was organized in collaboration with the College of Hospitality and Tourism Management Student Council (CHTM-SC), Arapaap Productions, the Junior Association of Hospitality Management Students (JAHMS), and the League of Tourism Students of the Philippines (LTSP), who worked together to ensure the program’s success.
The College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) Dean, Dr. Mark Alvin Lazaro, Assistant Dean Vincent Dela Cruz, Bachelor of Science and Tourism Management (BSTM) Program Coordinator Ma’am Lizette Ann P. Sia, Bachelor of Science in Hospitality Management (BSHM) Coordinator Vince Richard Gomez, and ARAPAAP Adviser Ms. Jenny Mae M. Lagman also extended strong support and leadership, which greatly guided the preparations for the rally.
To cap off the event, the atmosphere reached its peak as the CHTM Sharks filled the covered court with their department’s official Siklab cheer, embodying their spirit of unity and readiness for the upcoming sportsfest.| via Lovely Doroin/The FOREFRONT

[Image: MRPs Tree Planting]
[Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/extension/) [RDCES](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/rdces/) [Community Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/community-extension/)

# PRC On-site Caravan 2025 Lands with a Bang
[Image: PRC On-site Caravan 2025 Lands with a Bang]
[Alumni](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/alumni/) [CCS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ccs/) [IPDEA](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ipdea/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, May 9, 2025**
𝐏𝐑𝐎𝐅𝐄𝐒𝐒𝐈𝐎𝐍𝐀𝐋 𝐑𝐄𝐆𝐔𝐋𝐀𝐓𝐈𝐎𝐍 𝐂𝐎𝐌𝐌𝐈𝐒𝐒𝐈𝐎𝐍 (𝐏𝐑𝐂) 𝐎𝐍-𝐒𝐈𝐓𝐄 𝐒𝐄𝐑𝐕𝐈𝐂𝐄 𝐂𝐀𝐑𝐀𝐕𝐀𝐍
GORDON COLLEGE | OLONGAPO CITY, ZAMBALES
May 9, 2025
[Image: ✨]
It’s a wrap!
[Image: ✨]
The PRC On-Site Service Caravan at Gordon College was a resounding success, delivering a total of 549 services to Professionals in a single day. This initiative significantly improved access to essential PRC services within the local community—and is part of our ongoing commitment to support not only our Alumni but also to all Professionals.
Breakdown of Services Rendered:
• Renewal – 138
• Initial Registration – 60
• Duplicate – 11
• Certification – 170
• Authentication – 162
• Certificate of Special Order (COS) – 8
TOTAL – 549
A special acknowledgment to the dedicated PRC Committee for their exemplary service and leadership:
Ms. Elaine Perez Espela, Ms. Glenda B. Sapon (Team Leader), Ms. Joana Benigna Pabelonia, Mr. Joselito Dulay, and Mr. Angelo Pedro Hernandez.
We are also grateful to the student organizations for their invaluable support and active participation:
• College of Computer Studies Student Council (CCS SC)
• GCCCS Empowered League of Information Technology Education Students (ELITES)
Sincere appreciation goes to the VP for Institutional Planning, Development, and External Affairs, Dr. Kristin Joy A. Mendoza, for her instrumental role in the successful implementation of this initiative.
We would also like to express our heartfelt thanks to Mr. Joseph Angelo B. Pusing GCAAI President and Ms. Denise Lou Punzalan for spearheading this event, and to the Board of Trustees and Working Committee of the Gordon College Alumni Association Inc. (GCAAI) for their full support and commitment in making this initiative a success.
Special thanks to Dr. Imelda DP Soriano, College President, for her unwavering support in bringing services closer to the community.
This caravan is not just a service initiative—it’s our way of helping empower our alumni community as they continue their professional journeys.
Thank you to everyone who contributed to the success of the PRC On-Site Service Caravan. Together, we continue to broaden access to professional services and empower our community.

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/sucs-still-face-budget-cuts-in-proposed-2026-national-budget-kabataan

# SUCs still face budget cuts in proposed 2026 national budget – Kabataan
[Image: SUCs still face budget cuts in proposed 2026 national budget – Kabataan]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Saturday, October 18, 2025**
“Sa ibang institusyon o unibersidad [man], ang matapyasan ng budget para sa sektor ng edukasyon ay isang paraan upang mawalan ng karapatan ang mga magaaral na magkaroon ng kalidad na edukasyon,” a 4th Year BACOMM Student exclaimed.
Twenty-five State Universities and Colleges (SUCs) are still facing budget cuts amounting to Php 6.88 billion after Congress rejected amendments by Kabataan Partylist in its second-to-last hearing of the proposed 2026 national budget.
In a Facebook Post, youth-led Kabataan partylist condemns the House of Representative’s rejection of proposed amendments to House Bill (HB) No. 4058 or the proposed 2026 national budget that would have alleviated deficits to SUCs.
“Mariing tinututulan ng Kabataan Partylist ang pahayag ng Kongreso na sapat na raw ang dagdag-badyet sa SUCs at ang pagpondo sa 2022-2025 Free Higher Education (FHE) deficiencies. Danas ng bawat Iskolar ng Bayan ang hirap na magpatuloy sa kanilang pag-aaral dahil sa kakulangan ng pondo at suporta mula sa gobyerno,” the youth-led party-list said in a Facebook post.
Furthermore, in a report by Krixia Subingsubing for Philippine Daily Inquirer, twenty-five SUCs will be affected by budget cuts amounting to Php 6.88 billion. Three of these institutions are from Central Luzon including Aurora State College of Technology, Bulacan Agricultural State College, Central Luzon State University in Nueva Ecija and Philippine Merchant Marine Academy in Zambales.
Meanwhile, a group of school presidents and student regents from these institutions, led by Kabataan Party-list, has since issued a unity statement calling for Congress to restore budget cuts from the proposed national budget next year, citing “chronic underfunding” of the country’s education system.
According to the group, budget cuts compel SUCs to restrict admissions and compensate with “internally generated income from business ventures [and on] direct fee collection from students”.
As of October 10, the House has approved the proposed Php 6.7 trillion 2026 budget bill for 2026 following amendments. The budget is scheduled for a final reading in Congress on October 13 before congressional break. | via Lance Isaac Leon/The FOREFRONT
Sources: Kabataan Partylist/via FACEBOOK, Krixia Subingsubing/PHILIPPINE DAILY INQUIRER
[#InformingandEmpowering](https://www.facebook.com/hashtag/informingandempowering?__eep__=6&__tn__=*NK*F)

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gordon-college-gad-focal-point-system-conducts-benchmarking-visit-at-plm

# Gordon College GAD Focal Point System Conducts Benchmarking Visit at PLM
[Image: Gordon College GAD Focal Point System Conducts Benchmarking Visit at PLM]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, August 11, 2025**
Representatives from various departments of Gordon College, serving as members of the Gender and Development (GAD) Focal Point System, successfully concluded a benchmarking visit to Pamantasan ng Lungsod ng Maynila (PLM) last August 08, 2025. The activity aimed to strengthen the College’s GAD programs by learning from PLM’s best practices in promoting gender equality, inclusivity, and institutional development.
The delegation was warmly welcomed by PLM officials and staff. The visit featured comprehensive presentations on PLM’s Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) framework, gender-responsive policies, and student-centered initiatives. Delegates also toured PLM’s academic and administrative facilities, including specialized units dedicated to personnel and student support and welfare.
An open forum provided an opportunity for Gordon College representatives from different units to engage with PLM counterparts, exchanging strategies on implementing GAD-related projects, integrating gender perspectives in policies.
This activity reinforces Gordon College’s commitment to fostering a gender-responsive, inclusive, and equitable academic environment through continuous learning and partnership with other higher education institutions.

# GC RECOGINZED AS INTERNATIONALIZATION CHAMPION (ICONS) FOR ITS PERFORMANCE IN THE TIMES HIGHER EDUCATION IMPACT RANKINGS 2025
[Image: GC RECOGINZED AS INTERNATIONALIZATION CHAMPION (ICONS) FOR ITS PERFORMANCE IN THE TIMES HIGHER EDUCATION IMPACT RANKINGS 2025]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, December 10, 2025**
Today, the College was honored by the Commission on Higher Education for our outstanding achievements in global rankings, sustainability, ASEAN awareness, and internationalization during the Internationalization Champions of Nation-Building and Sustainability (ICONS) Awards 2025, held on December 10, 2025 (Wednesday), from 1:00 PM to 9:00 PM at the Philippine International Convention Center (PICC) in Pasay City.
The College was recognized for its high-impact sustainability initiatives, commendable performance in international rankings and ratings, pioneering efforts to advance internationalization, and valuable contributions to United Nation’s Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).
This recognition is the result of our Internationalization initiative, based on the released ranking results for this year in the Times Higher Education Impact Rankings 2025.
The College extends its gratitude to Hon. Rolen C. Paulino Jr., City Mayor; Hon. Cristabelle Marie C. Paulino, Committee on Education Chairperson; Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, College President; Dr. Kristin Joy A. Mendoza, Vice President for Institutional Planning and External Affairs; Ms. Precious S. De Castro, Coordinator of the International Affairs Unit; other Vice Presidents, BOT Members, College Officials, Management Council Members, faculty members, administrative personnel, and all other stakeholders who contributed to this achievement.

# Gordon College Receives 21 Certificates of Accreditation at NNQAA 2026 Summit
[Image: Gordon College Receives 21 Certificates of Accreditation at NNQAA 2026 Summit]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, March 6, 2026**
Gordon College proudly received Certificates of Accreditation for its 21 ALCUCOA-accredited programs during the NNQAA 2nd Quality Assurance Summit and Conferment Ceremony for Accredited Programs and Institutions 2026, with the theme “Responsible Future: Quality Assurance in Action.”
The recognition was conferred by the National Network of Quality Assurance Agencies (NNQAA), Inc., led by Chairman Dr. Raymundo P. Arcega, during the summit held March 6, 2026 at The Heritage Hotel Manila.
The award was received by Dr. Imelda D.P. Soriano, College President; Dr. Kristin Joy A. Mendoza, Vice President for Institutional Planning, Development and External Affairs; and Mr. Joseph Angelo B. Pusing, Officer-in-Charge of the Center for Quality Assurance and Accreditation, representing Gordon College.
These recognitions highlight the College’s continued commitment to quality education, academic excellence, and institutional development.
Level III Accredited Programs
1. Bachelor of Science in Business Administration – Financial Management
2. Bachelor of Science in Business Administration – Human Resource Management
3. Bachelor of Science in Business Administration – Marketing Management
4. Bachelor of Science in Customs Administration
5. Bachelor of Secondary Education – Mathematics
6. Bachelor of Secondary Education – Social Studies
7. Bachelor of Science in Nursing
Level II Accredited Programs
1. Bachelor of Elementary Education
2. Bachelor of Science in Accountancy
3. Bachelor of Science in Computer Science
4. Bachelor of Secondary Education – English
5. Bachelor of Secondary Education – Science
6. Bachelor of Science in Information Technology
Level I Accredited Programs
1. Bachelor of Arts in Communication
2. Bachelor of Early Childhood Education
3. Bachelor of Secondary Education – Filipino
4. Bachelor of Science in Hospitality Management
5. Bachelor of Science in Midwifery
6. Bachelor of Science in Tourism Management
Candidate Status Programs
1. Bachelor of Culture and Arts Education
2. Bachelor of Physical Education
The College extends its deepest gratitude to Hon. Rolen C. Paulino Jr., City Mayor; Hon. Cristabelle Marie C. Paulino, Chairperson of the Committee on Education; Dr. Imelda D.P. Soriano, College President; the City Officials, the Board of Trustees, Vice Presidents, College Officials, Deans, Assistant Deans, Program Chairs, Management Council Members, Faculty Members, Administrative Personnel, and all our stakeholders for their continued support in strengthening Gordon College’s pursuit of quality assurance and academic excellence.

# Gordon College Joins National Simultaneous Earthquake Drill
[Image: Gordon College Joins National Simultaneous Earthquake Drill]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, March 12, 2026**
LOOK | Students and personnel of Gordon College (GC) gathered earlier this afternoon, March 12, at Tapinac Oval Track as part of the National Simultaneous Earthquake Drill (NSED).
The earthquake drill simultaneously occurred at 3:30pm today in public and private institutions across the country to strengthen community resilience against a possible disaster.
NSED is practiced every quarter — totaling four times in a year — to establish nationwide earthquake preparedness. This is in preparation for possible earthquakes in the future including the “Big One”, a projected magnitude 7.2 earthquake predicted to hit Metro Manila and affect nearby provinces as the West Valley Fault is ripe for movement. | via Lance Isaac Leon/The FOREFRONT
Photos by Lheeon Owen Dela Rosa, Mikhaela Sagun

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-releases-statement-over-alleged-p19-million-overpayment

# GC releases statement over alleged P19-million overpayment
[Image: GC releases statement over alleged P19-million overpayment]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, January 5, 2026**
“The College wishes to clarify that funds received under the Free Higher Education Program are meticulously allocated and budgeted which undergoes strict audit by appropriate national government agencies. All collections approved were allocated transparently for the operations and development of the college,” Gordon College (GC) stated.
Following the publication of a Commission on Audit (COA) report cited by Bilyonaryo News Channel, GC released an official statement addressing the alleged P19-million overpayment under the Free Higher Education (FHE) Program.
The institution clarifies that all funds received were meticulously allocated and budgeted, emphasizing that all approved funds were used transparently for the institution’s operations and development.
According to the COA report, there are three (3) Local Universities and Colleges (LUCs) with ineligibilities and excessive fees due to loss in control mechanism of the Commission on Higher Education (CHED) Central Office and Unified Students Financial Assistance System for Tertiary Education (UniFAST) Billing Unit, amounting to more than P295-million.
The three institutions identified were the Tangub City Global College (TCGC), Pamantasan ng Cabuyao (PNC), and Gordon College (GC).
For GC, COA reported a total amount of P19,260,800 in questioned fees. The audit findings pointed to a mismatch between GC’s definition of a laboratory room and COA’s definition of an Audio-Visual Room (AVR).
Meanwhile, PNC listed an overpayment amounting to P55,818,690, which focuses on the leadership training, student partnerships, student publication and newsletters, and remedial programs under the Development category—being the highest recipient.
On the other hand, TCGC recorded the highest alleged overpayment at P220,706,895, with cultural fees accounting for the largest portion of the amount.
Furthermore, COA noted that this was not the first time CHED had been called out for overpayments under the FHE Program.
Audit observations from 2021 to 2022 already pointed to excess budgeting, while the 2024 report cited lapses in verification processes of the CHED Central Office and UniFAST Secretariat, resulting in the continued cycle of observations and disallowances since fiscal year 2022.
As part of its general recommendations, COA directed the three institutions to return the overpayments made by CHED, or to avail themselves of other appropriate modes of settlement of accounts in accordance with the existing laws and regulations.
In response, GC reiterated its commitment to accountability and transparency, emphasizing its willingness to cooperate with auditing authorities and comply with lawful processes. | via Mikhaela Sagun/The FOREFRONT

# GC honors DASArving alumni, highlights iconic “first-times” in 4th DASA
[Image: GC honors DASArving alumni, highlights iconic “first-times” in 4th DASA]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, October 14, 2025**
SMX OLONGAPO CENTRAL— The 4th Distinguished Alumni Service Awards (DASA) 2025 gathered alumni who paved innovation and inspiration for Gordon College on October 11, held at SMX Olongapo Central.
Mr. Joseph Pusing, former Gordon College Alumni Association Inc. (GCAAI) President, proudly presented all the first-time projects that happened on the initiative of giving back to their alma mater: Alumni cards, Financial support for the student council and organizations, Cosme Y. Sering Library, and a fifty thousand cash donation for the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) Department for the extension of rooms for accreditation.
“One of the highlights of our term is the donation of the alumni association’s newest student lounge, which was blessed on August 15, 2025. This will not be possible without the help and guidance of everyone,” he stated.
Also, Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, the college president, exclaimed in her keynote speech that the world needs someone like them who wagers unselfish services in a world full of selfishness.
Furthermore, Ms. Rose Anne Cañeda Dumalo, alumna from the Bachelor of Science in Education – English (BSEd-English) Batch 2015, expressed in her speech that titles and recognition will not define us but the people that we serve along the way.
“Higit sa pagiging magaling ay ang pagiging mabuti. You may be the best in the field, but if you lack character, people will remember how you treated them more than how well you did your job,” she added.
Moreover, Ms. Ellinor T. Manaois-Yco, Bachelor of Science in Nursing (BSN) Batch 2011; Mr. Arben R. Cauilan, Bachelor of Science in Customs Administration (BSCA) Batch 2011; Nikki Antonette Chieng Carlos, Bachelor of Science in Tourism (BSTM); and Mr. Willy R. Antigo, Master of Arts in Education (MAEd) Batch 2019, were awarded with recognitions and plaques for their outstanding service and contributions.
In addition, Ms. Lovelyn P. Ceralde, DASA 2025 Overall Chairperson, extended her warm welcome and gratitude to the alumni, stakeholders, and those who have been bringing their best to provide a more quality education and experience inside the institution.
Finally, the program ended with an oath-taking for the newly elected GCAAI officers and board of trustees for the fiscal year 2025 – 2026. | via John Patrick Mateo/The FOREFRONT

# HISTORY IN THE MAKING: GCIANS CONQUER THE GLOBAL GAMING STAGE!
[Image: HISTORY IN THE MAKING: GCIANS CONQUER THE GLOBAL GAMING STAGE!]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, February 17, 2026**
The legend returns, and our very own alumni are leading the charge! Gordon College is bursting with pride as we celebrate the official launch of God of War: Sons of Sparta on the PS5!
This isn’t just a win for the gaming world; it’s a massive milestone for our institution. We are incredibly honored to recognize the brilliant minds from our BSEMC and BSCS programs who collaborated with Santa Monica Studio, Mega Cat Studios, and PlayStation to bring this retro-inspired masterpiece to life.
A huge round of applause for our talented Alumni:
BSEMC ALUMNI:
* Daniele Victor R. Marilag
* Marianne Silver
* Mark Angelo A. Quindara
* Roselle Gaw
BSCS ALUMNI:
* Ron Benedict A. Panes
* Prince Kyle Dela Cruz
From the labs of Gordon College to the global credits of a legendary franchise, you have proven that Filipino talent is world-class. Your hard work, technical skill, and creativity serve as a beacon for every student dreaming of making their mark in the tech and arts industry.
Thank you for showing the world what it means to be a GCian. The entire Gordon College community stands behind you!
Padayon, Developers!

# CEAS Mentors Women’s Doubles Clear Past CHTM Sharks for the Win
[Image: CEAS Mentors Women’s Doubles Clear Past CHTM Sharks for the Win]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, September 17, 2025**
TRICKSHOT BADMINTON COURT—Flora Amor Francisco and Vherna Lei Castañada of the College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS) made a powerful smash to victory in the Women’s Double category against the College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) with a score of 21-10.
The Trickshot Badminton Court echoed with loud smashes and cheers as Francisco and Castañada dominated the game, leaving the CHTM Sharks with no chance at victory. The first set ended decisively, 21-4.
Francisco expressed her thrill and happiness during the interview as they were the first to win gold for the team.
“We know na ‘yon ‘yung way para lumakas ‘yung loob ng ibang ka-team namin, para lumaban at manalo para maibalik sa CEAS Badminton ang overall. Also, I promised before I graduated na maibalik ang overall champ sa CEAS Badminton,” the victor shared.
Francisco also expressed the challenges they faced in synchronizing their movements during matches, emphasizing how it is one of the most important aspects of playing doubles—where victory depends on two players moving as one.
In the elimination round, the College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) Sharks battled fiercely with the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) Wizards, with the Sharks taking the win.
In the semifinals, the College of Computer Studies (CCS) Phoenix faced the CEAS Mentors, while the College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) Tigers battled the CHTM Sharks, setting up a championship showdown between CHTM and CEAS.
For the final standings, CAHS Tigers secured third place, CCS Phoenix finished in fourth place, and CBA Wizards settled into fifth place. | via Jeliza Tadena/The FOREFRONT
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# Suspension of Transactions
[Image: Suspension of Transactions]
**Thursday, December 11, 2025**
ANNOUNCEMENT  
December 11, 2025
**Suspension of Transactions**
Please be informed that all College transactions will be suspended from Monday, December 15, to Tuesday, December 16, 2025, due to the Strategic Planning for all College Personnel.
Additionally, transactions at the College Registrar’s Office will be suspended earlier, on Friday, December 12, 2025, for their attendance to Assembly of Registrars in Region 3.
All transactions will resume on Wednesday, December 17, 2025.
Thank you for your understanding.


### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/announcement/online-application-for-gordon-college-admission-test-gcat

# ONLINE APPLICATION for Gordon College Admission Test (GCAT)
[Image: ONLINE APPLICATION for Gordon College Admission Test (GCAT)]
**Thursday, November 27, 2025**
[Image: 📌]
**ONLINE APPLICATION for Gordon College Admission Test (GCAT)**
[Image: 📅]
First Semester A.Y. 2026–2027
[Image: 🗓️]
Application Period: November 27, 2025 – February 27, 2026
[Image: 📝]
GCAT Starts: January 26, 2026 – June 30, 2026
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
[Image: 📂]
**APPLICATION REQUIREMENTS**
• Senior High School ID or Any Valid ID Card
• PSA Authenticated Birth Certificate
• Recent Good Moral Character Certificate
• Academic Records
– Senior High School applicants: Grade 11 grades
– Transferees: Transcript of Records (TOR) or Certificate of Grades
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
[Image: 🖥️]
**APPLICATION PROCEDURE**
[Image: 🔺]
Basahin muna ang buong steps bago i-click ang portal link.
[Image: 🟢]
STEP 1. CREATE ONLINE PORTAL ACCOUNT
1. Go to the Gordon College Admission Portal
2. Click “Sign up”
3. Fill in: email, given name, last name, date of birth, contact number, desired password, and type of student (New, Transferee, Second Courser)
[Image: 📌]
Notes:
• Use a valid and existing email address.
• Make sure all details are correct.
• DO NOT create multiple accounts (ground for disapproval).
• Remember your email and password.
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
[Image: 🟢]
STEP 2. UPLOADING OF DOCUMENTS
1. Upload scanned copies or clear photos of the required documents (≤ 2MB each):
o Profile photo
– 2×2 colored picture, white background
– Formal pose, collared top
– No eyeglasses or accessories
– Taken within 6 months before filing
o Good Moral Character Certificate
o PSA Authenticated Birth Certificate
o Senior High School ID or Valid ID
o Academic Records (Grade 11 grades for SHS, TOR for transferees)
2. Click “Continue with Sign Up” to finalize.
Important: Always double-check to ensure that all documents have been successfully uploaded.
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
[Image: 🟢]
STEP 3. LOG IN TO YOUR ACCOUNT
1. Go to the portal login page.
2. Enter your registered email and password.
3. You will be directed to your Dashboard, which displays the status of your application.
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
[Image: 🟢]
STEP 4. PROFILING
1. On the Menu, click “Profile.”
2. Fill out all forms (Personal Info, Family Background, Desired Programs).
3. Read and check the Data Privacy Notice.
4. Review and click “Submit.”
5. Under Confirmation, click “Finalized Enlistment.”
o This step is required before you can proceed to Exam Scheduling.
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
[Image: 🟢]
STEP 5. GCAT SCHEDULE
• The schedule will be posted on your portal account.
• Once posted, you may download and print:
o GCAT Schedule Slip
o Student Information Sheet
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
[Image: 🟢]
STEP 6. APPLICATION INTERVIEW
• Within 3 days, your GCAT result will be posted in your portal.
• As soon as the results are posted, you will be scheduled for an interview by the Program Coordinator.
• Please come on the scheduled interview. The interview is part of the requirements in assessing if you will be accepted into the program.
• On the day of interview, bring the following:
o Copy of grades for Grade 12, 1st Semester or TOR for transferees.
o And other requirements as stated by the Program Coordinator.
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
[Image: 🟢]
STEP 7. CONFIRMATION OF ADMISSION
• After the interview, kindly check your portal to see if you are admitted to the program you have applied for.
• You have to click the “confirm” button in your portal. Clicking the “confirm” button will ensure that you will enroll at the college.
• After confirmation, stay tuned for announcements for Enlistment. prepare all requirements for enrolment.
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
[Image: 📌]
**WHAT TO BRING ON TEST DAY**
1. GCAT Schedule Slip
2. Student Information Sheet
3. 2×2 picture with white background
4. Senior High School or Valid ID Card
5. Pencil and Pen
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
[Image: ❗]
**Important Note:**
All applications shall be submitted online only. Please make sure to read and complete all steps before clicking the portal link.
[Image: 🔗]
Apply here: [https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/gca/student/](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/gca/student/?fbclid=IwZXh0bgNhZW0CMTAAYnJpZBExYmtONXprRWlaY05XUDNlanNydGMGYXBwX2lkEDIyMjAzOTE3ODgyMDA4OTIAAR5Y5hG2YA_AI__1lMPwTeyW8CLviRK6VuTQPPcj-06-Tfru_BR9tRn1-SqA1Q_aem_60uz7kL2AWjV46jhREbXFg)


### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-ssc-benchmarks-student-leadership-and-governance-among-heis
