# NAME PROTECTED: Agaton and Cerilles Among the Philippines’ Most Outstanding
[Image: NAME PROTECTED: Agaton and Cerilles Among the Philippines’ Most Outstanding]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, August 27, 2025**
Two students from Aduana – Gordon College (GC) Chapter were recognized as the Most Outstanding BSCA Students of the Philippines for 2025 at an awards ceremony held by the Philippine Society of Customs Administration Students (PSCAS) Nationals on August 16 at Wind Residences, Tagaytay City.
Kris Rean Agaton was named the Top 1 Most Outstanding BSCA Student, while Renmmelle Kyle Cerilles secured the Top 3 position.
AGATON’S CUSTOMized JOURNEY
For Agaton, receiving the top honor was a moment of profound shock and validation.
She acknowledged experiencing personal doubt at the moment when she was nominated and considered her lack of extracurricular activities as a drawback compared to her fellow nominees across the country.
“I initially thought my chances of making it into the Top 10 were slim. That’s why, when I received the message that I ranked number one, I was overwhelmed with happiness and disbelief,” she added.
Furthermore, Agaton shared that her mother has always been the biggest source of motivation, as she’s been pushing her to move outside her comfort zone.
She revealed that, in her second year, she consistently declined opportunities for national competitions due to a “fear of failure.”
“Why not give it a try? It’s your last chance. I know you don’t regret turning down the chances before, but at least take this one as something you can look back on in the future,” her mother assured.
This encouragement helped her overcome her fear of failure.
CE-REALEST AND DEDICATION
On the other hand, Cerilles’s journey to the Top 3 position was a story of significant personal transformation. He admitted that he was far from what many would call ‘outstanding’ and did not always take academics as seriously as he should have.
“Honestly, if you look back at past years, I was far from what many would call ‘outstanding.’ I used to be the type of student who would rather stand outside the classroom,” Cerilles shared.
It was deliberate of him to give his best in whatever he did, and this eventually resulted in a leadership position at PSCAS-GC that took him to positions he never expected.
“This award is not just a recognition of what I achieved, but a reflection of how far I’ve come. From someone unsure of their place to someone willing to go beyond their borders,” he reflected.
Similarly, Cerilles attributed his success to the lessons and sacrifices of his parents.
He sees them as his “greatest mentors,” with his mother’s resilience and his father’s resourcefulness shaping his character and approach to challenges.
“They reminded me that success is never about individual effort alone, it is built upon the values, discipline, and character that we learn from those who raised us,” he emphasized.
When the two were asked what they felt was the most valuable piece of advice to give to other aspiring students of the Bachelor of Science in Customs Administration, both Agaton and Cerilles returned with bits of wisdom based primarily on their own experiences.
The advice given by Agaton focused on being aware of oneself and being focused.
The cluster I valedictorian urged students to remember their “why”—the reason they started—and to make that their anchor.
“Your journey is your own. Stay focused, be patient with yourself, and trust that your efforts—no matter how big or small—are leading you somewhere meaningful,” she reminded.
Meanwhile, Cerilles’ message was a call to “move beyond borders.”
Using the metaphor of an “aduana” (customs), he encouraged students to be brave enough to step out of their comfort zones.
Whether it is joining a student organization or taking academics more seriously, it brings one closer to discovering one’s true potential, he advised the students that growth only happens when you dare to cross those borders. | via Princess Alliyah Danday/The FOREFRONT

[Image: GC 27th Founding Anniversary Schedule]
**Monday, February 23, 2026**
Gordon College proudly invites the entire GC community to take part in the 27th Founding Anniversary Celebration happening from February 24 to February 26.
Get ready for three days filled with meaningful activities, campus spirit, and memorable moments as we come together to celebrate our legacy, achievements, and the vibrant community that makes GC truly exceptional.
Mark your calendars and join us as we celebrate this milestone in Gordon College history.

# 𝟗 𝐆𝐂𝐈𝐀𝐍𝐒 𝐏𝐀𝐒𝐒𝐄𝐃 𝐂𝐏𝐀𝐋𝐄 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟓; 𝐆𝐂 𝐑𝐄𝐂𝐎𝐑𝐃𝐒 𝟓𝟔.𝟐𝟓% 𝐏𝐀𝐒𝐒𝐈𝐍𝐆 𝐑𝐀𝐓𝐄
[Image: 𝟗 𝐆𝐂𝐈𝐀𝐍𝐒 𝐏𝐀𝐒𝐒𝐄𝐃 𝐂𝐏𝐀𝐋𝐄 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟓; 𝐆𝐂 𝐑𝐄𝐂𝐎𝐑𝐃𝐒 𝟓𝟔.𝟐𝟓% 𝐏𝐀𝐒𝐒𝐈𝐍𝐆 𝐑𝐀𝐓𝐄]
[CBA](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/cba/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Saturday, June 7, 2025**
Gordon College (GC) College of Business and Accountrancy (CBA) records 9 new Certified Public Accountant (CPA) in the recently held May 2025 Certified Public Accountant Licensure Exam (CPALE).
The institution aimed an overall perfomance of 56.25%, which surpassed the national rating of 33.11%, with a total of 9 out of 16 examinees joining 3,156 new CPAs in the country.
Out of 13 first time examinees, 8 passed the examination, with 2 considered as conditional. Additionally, among the 3 repeaters, 1 passed while another 1 was classified as conditional.
The licensure examination was administered by the Professional Regulation Comission (PRC) from May 25 to 27 nationwide.
Source: Professional Regulation Commission (PRC)

# 3 studes earn top spots in CFMS and CMMS exams; GC produces new CPACE-certified undergrads
[Image: 3 studes earn top spots in CFMS and CMMS exams; GC produces new CPACE-certified undergrads]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, December 23, 2025**
Francis Jerome De Jesus and Belynda Apog, 4th-year Bachelor of Science in Business Administration Major in Financial Management students, ranked 4th and 5th, respectively, in their assessment examinations. Meanwhile, Marketing Management major Michaella Soliven secured the 10th spot of her own.
In a post, the Center for Professional Advancement and Continuing Education, Inc. (CPACE Philippines) announced the passers of the Batch 25 Certified Financial Management Specialist (CFMS)® and Certified Marketing Management Specialist (CMMS)® assessment examinations on Thursday, December 18, and Friday, December 19, respectively.
De Jesus, 90.42%, and Apog, 90%, both broke into the Top 10 for the CFMS® assessment exam. On the flip side, Soliven cleared the CMMS® assessment with a rating of 86.67%.
Furthermore, 78 additional Financial Management from the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) students cleared the assessment, bringing the institutional passing rate to 76.70%, while the results also saw one more taker pass the CMMS® exam.
CPACE Philippines totaled an overall passing rate of 69.88% and 80% for CFMS® and CMMS® examinations, respectively.
The assessment examinations were conducted via online and in-person formats, including Gordon College (GC) – Annex Bldg., from November 28 through December 13. | via Keane Rafael Abdon/The FOREFRONT

# LCUAA 2025 Welcomes Rainy Sunday Afternoon
[Image: LCUAA 2025 Welcomes Rainy Sunday Afternoon]
[LCUAA](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/lcuaa/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, October 19, 2025**
The eagerly anticipated week-long 14th LCUAA National Games officially kicked off today at the City of San Jose del Monte (CSJDM) Sports Complex.
Cheers and applause filled the venue as the opening ceremony began with a parade featuring all 27 participating colleges, each led by their institution’s gold medalists.
Following the parade was the introduction of the LCUAA 2025 representative officers, a doxology by the Sining Tanglawan ng San Jose del Monte Performing Group, the singing of the National Anthem and the San Jose del Monte city hymn, and an energetic dance performance by the San Joseño World Class Talents troupe.
City College of San Jose del Monte (CCSJDM) College President Dr. Roger S. Ambida delivered the opening remarks, warmly welcoming delegates to the institution.
Hon. Arturo B. Robes then gave the keynote address, encouraging athletes to build a brighter and more empowered future.
Additionally, Dr. Lora L. Yusi, CHED Region 3 Director IV, gave an inspiring speech, highlighting the significance of LCUAA 2025 as a testament to the growth of local universities: “We see scholars who dream larger than themselves.”
The competitive events officially begin tomorrow, October 20, 2025.
Stay tuned to the official Facebook page of The Forefront for updates on the 14th LCUAA National Games. | via Alyssa Marie Alcantara / The FOREFRONT

# Galaxies of Unity: CCS Students Explore New Worlds Together
[Image: Galaxies of Unity: CCS Students Explore New Worlds Together]
[CCS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ccs/) [ForeFront](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/forefront/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, September 14, 2025**
ASINAN COVERED COURT, OLONGAPO CITY—The College of Computer Studies (CCS) of Gordon College launched its academic year with a universe of energy at the annual General Orientation and Acquaintance Party, “Galaxies of Unity: Exploring New Worlds Together,” held on September 13, 2025, at the Asinan Covered Court.
The event aims to transform into a constellation of colors, performances, advocacies, and camaraderie, welcoming both new and returning students into a community built on diversity, inclusion, and shared growth.
Furthermore, carrying the banner “Innovate, Include, Inspire: Pioneering Equality and Diversity in the AI-Driven World,” the orientation extended beyond the usual acquaintance party.
The event, spearheaded by Dean Ronnie Luy and Assistant Dean Armilyn Martinez with the support of the CCS faculty and coordinators, united students from the three CCS undergraduate programs—Bachelor of Science in Computer Science (BSCS), Bachelor of Science in Information Technology (BSIT), and Bachelor of Science in Entertainment and Multimedia Computing (BSEMC)—in celebrating inclusivity, teamwork, and talent.
“This event is not just for acquaintances, but also for orientation and presentation of our organizations and sportsfest participants. We want freshmen to feel welcome and informed, and for everyone to know the CCS community they belong to,” shared Ms. Yheng Miel Sanchez, CCS faculty.
At the heart of the program was the introduction of eight planetary teams, each representing a social cause: Lumora for Accessibility and Compassion (Persons with Disabilities), Veltraxis for Collaboration (Ethnicity and Race), Aurelia for Empathy (Senior Citizens), Cryonex for Innovation (AI and Robotics), Floraxis for Sustainability and Culture (Cultural Heritage), Kahelion for Resilience and Advocacy (Frontline Workers), Nyxara for Identity (LGBTQIA+), and Zenthara for Equity (Women Empowerment and Gender Equity).
Moreover, the day opened with an invocation, welcome remarks, and the general assembly before teams were formally revealed. Excitement built as each segment unfolded: cosplay presentations highlighted student creativity and confidence; production numbers filled the venue with music and dance; interactive games such as Constellation Connection, Bring Me, Inclusion Mosaic, One Word Explains All, Voices of the Galaxy, and The Matrix sparked laughter and teamwork; entrepreneurial booths offered handmade crochets, stickers, badges, and keychains; Echoes of Unity, the team chant presentations, raised school spirit; and raffle draws added surprise and fun throughout the day.
“Okay lang naman yung event, nakakapagod pero enjoyable. Pinakagusto ko yung cosplay, because makikita mo yung confidence and effort ng mga sumali,” said Jerry Andrew Famanila, CCS Student.
The cheers peaked with the introduction of players for CCS Sportsfest 2025, closing with the fiery chant, “Go CCS Phoenix!”. Student organizations, including the CCS Student Council, Society of Programming Enthusiasts in Computer Science (SPECS), Empowered League of Information Technology Education Students (ELITES), and Innovative Multimedia Artists Guild of Empowered Students (IMAGES), ensured the program’s smooth flow by managing venue setup, technical aspects, and coordination.
Between lively intermissions, the spotlight turned to academic achievements. The Dean’s Lister Awarding Ceremony honored the Top 10 achievers per program (BSCS, BSIT, BSEMC–Digital Animation, BSEMC–Game Development) and the Top 50 overall Dean’s Listers for A.Y. 2024–2025. Special recognitions, including Top Cosplayers, Best Production Number, and Winning Team Planet, added a celebratory flair, affirming that CCS nurtures both artistry and innovation.
In addition, John Derick De Leon, BSIT Student, shared that everything went according to the plan—which makes it very satisfying.
“Medyo mainit at may mga segment na biglaan pero doon lumalabas yung creativity ng students. Kitang-kita rin yung teamwork. Favorite ko yung part na bigla kaming pinag-drawing sa Inclusion Mosaic,” Mateo B. Bandilio, BSEMC student, shared.
Moreover, while the day brimmed with laughter, its deeper purpose was clear: to introduce freshmen to the CCS culture, strengthen bonds among students, and celebrate diversity as a source of strength. By merging advocacies with artistry and recognition with entertainment, the event became more than a kick-off celebration. It reaffirmed CCS’s mission to innovate, include, and inspire while promoting equality across differences.
As acknowledgments and final remarks drew the day to a close, ‘Galaxies of Unity’ stood as more than an acquaintance party. Guided by their advocacies, CCS students are set to carry the spirit of unity into the rest of the academic year, a reminder that while they may belong to different ‘planets’ of identity, talent, and cause, together they can form a universe of their own | Featured by Charyll Mae Cabungcal/The FOREFRONT
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/announcement/3rd-dasa-call-nomination

# 3rd Distinguished Alumni Service Award: Call for Nomination 2024
[Image: 3rd Distinguished Alumni Service Award: Call for Nomination 2024]
[Alumni](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/alumni/)
**Wednesday, January 24, 2024**
## THE 3RD GORDON COLLEGE DISTINGUISHED ALUMNI SERVICE AWARD (GC-DASA) FOR THE YEAR 2024
**GENERAL INFORMATION AND GUIDELINES**
Pursuant to Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc. (GCAAI) Board Resolution No. 4, series of 2022, the GCAAI establishes the **DISTINGUISHED ALUMNI SERVICE AWARD (DASA)** to honor Gordon College graduates who have excelled both in their professional and personal careers and who have served as an example to other alumni, students, and the community, both locally and globally. Starting this year, the GCAAI Board of Trustees will recognize the exemplary service and distinguished achievements that brought honor to the Institution and have made significant contributions to society. The GC-DASA recipients will exemplify the core values that the Institution uphold since its establishment: **Exceptional Character, Remarkable Excellence, And Unselfish Service.**
The GC-DASA shall be the highest honor bestowed by the Alumni Association and will be an event to be celebrated annually as part of the GC Founding Anniversary and/or GC Alumni Homecoming.
## GENERAL GUIDELINES:
1. Only graduates of Gordon College (or GC, formerly Olongapo City Colleges) are eligible for the GC-DASA. The nominee shall be a member of the Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc. as confirmed by its Board.
2. Any GC graduate, faculty member, or stakeholders may make nominations for the GC-DASA. Self–nominations are accepted. The nominee shall accomplish the Official Nomination Form to be provided by the GCAAI. No limitation will be placed on the total number of nominations accepted for consideration.
3. The nominee shall have no pending administrative or criminal case upon nomination. He/she shall exhibit a high degree of character & integrity.
4. Only one recipient per college/program will be awarded. In case the college/program will have no nominees/recipients, the awarding ceremony will still commence. Likewise, the GCAAI Board of Trustees can directly, without prejudice to the respective college, nominate and evaluate for such an award though the college/program have already a candidate.
5. Only living candidates are considered. In the event of the death of a candidate between notification of the award and the award ceremony, the award may be given posthumously with the permission of the family and the GCAAI. In the event that the awardee declines to accept the award and/or fails to attend the awarding ceremonies, the GCAAI reserves the right to withdraw the award.
6. A GC graduate sitting as a member of the selection committee or a member of the GCAAI Board of Trustees is not eligible to receive the award. This individual is eligible for nomination after a one-year waiting period from the date he/she step down from his/her role.
7. Recipients are only eligible for one GC-DASA in a lifetime.
8. The recipients will receive a plaque or certificate of recognition and will be posted on the official FB page of the GCAAI. It will announce the annual recipients through various methods and honor each recipient with the appropriate recognition.
9. If for any reason, the GCAAI is not able to conduct the Awards as planned (including due to tampering, unauthorized intervention, fraud, technical difficulties, pandemic, or any other causes beyond the control of the organizer which impact the administration, security, fairness, integrity or proper conduct of the Awards), then they reserve the right in their sole discretion to make any reasonable variation to the Awarding Ceremony.
10. The Nomination Committee and/or GCAAI Board of Trustees may disqualify any nominee that they reasonably determine has breached any of the terms and conditions or otherwise engaged in unlawful or other improper conduct that may bring the Awards into disrepute or jeopardize the fair and proper conduct of the Awards.
11. Other guidelines may be promulgated as decided by the GCAAI Board of Trustees.
## NOMINATION COMMITTEE:
The Nomination Committee (NC) may be organized by the Dean/OIC in every college and/or the GCAAI. The committee must be composed of at least three (3) members from their respective colleges. The total count of committee members should be an odd number. The chairperson of the NC shall be the Dean or its duly authorized representative and for the GCAAI, any member can initiate such nomination. No member of the NC must be related within the second degree of consanguinity or affinity to any of the nominees/candidates for awards. If the Dean/OIC is related within the second degree of consanguinity or affinity to any of the candidates for awards, the Dean/OIC must inhibit him/herself from participating in the process. The approval shall come from the person next in rank.
The NC shall:
1. Abide by the criteria set forth by the GCAAI.
2. For nominees endorsed by the College, it shall issue a certificate of authenticity to all the documents that will be submitted to the GCAAI.
3. Deliberate on the qualifications of the nominees for the awards based on the criteria and the documents submitted.
4. Recommend to the GCAAI Board of Trustees or its Alumni Association President the result of nomination for approval.
5. Recommend to the GCAAI Board of Trustees or its Alumni Association President the resolution on any related issue that may arise from the results of the awards.
6. Ensure that the guidelines stipulated herein are followed.
## NOMINATION AND SELECTION PROCESS:
1. Complete the online/hard copy Nomination Form. This also forms part of the terms and conditions.
2. Nominators will receive an auto-generated copy via email of what they submitted, i.e., the nomination form including any attachments. In any case, it is not available, the NC may determine what means of notification it will conduct to the nominators to confirm the submission of the nomination form and its attachments.
3. Nominees and nominators will be contacted by the NC/GCAAI within two days of receipt of the nomination to acknowledge receipt.
4. A nominee may be asked to clarify or submit further information and evidence to assist the NC/GCAAI in assessing the nomination at any stage of the selection process.
5. The NC/GCAAI may also seek verification of the nominee’s achievements from their current or previous employer or other relevant individual or organization representative. By being nominated for this Award, the nominees are giving consent to the NC/GCAAI to contact these individuals to verify nominations.
6. Nominations will be reviewed and shortlisted by the NC/GCAAI and nominees who have not been shortlisted will be notified. The GC-DASA candidates will be asked to submit a brief profile for this purpose. The candidate shall garner a total score of 60 points to be included in the shortlist. If a nominee cannot meet the required points, he/she is automatically excluded from the shortlist.
7. Every member of the NC will recommend/endorse the final candidate to the GCAAI President and then, the GCAAI Board of Trustees, sitting as Awards Selection Committee, will issue a confirmation through a Board Resolution. The report on the results of the NC shall be signed by all members of the committee and certified by the chairperson.
8. In any case that such protest will be filed during the confirmation of the final candidates, the final decision on the protest shall be decided by the GCAAI Board of Trustees. The GCAAI Board of Trustees’ decision is final on all matters relating to the nomination process and the selection of award recipients, and they are not required to provide feedback as to why a nomination was unsuccessful.
**CRITERIA:**
|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |

# Blessing of Gordon College Annex Building
[Image: Blessing of Gordon College Annex Building]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, August 11, 2025**
Pormal na binasbasan ang bagong Annex Building ng Gordon College ngayong Agosto 11, 2025, sa pangunguna ni Rev. Fr. Roque Villanueva.
Dumalo sa makabuluhang seremonya sina Mayor Atty. Rolen C. Paulino Jr., BCDA Director Rolen C. Paulino, Gordon College President Dr. Imelda D.P. Soriano, mga Konsehal ng Lungsod, at ilan pang opisyal, kasama ang mga estudyante, guro, at kawani ng Gordon College bilang pagsuporta sa makasaysayang pagbubukas ng bagong gusali.
Mahigit sa tatlong libong karagdagang estudyante ang makikinabang sa bagong gusali.
Itinakda ang pagbubukas ng Annex Building kasabay ng pasimula ng klase ngayong Academic Year 2025–2026, bilang bahagi ng patuloy na pagtugon ng lokal na pamahalaan at pamunuan ng kolehiyo sa lumalaking pangangailangan para sa dekalidad at makabagong pasilidad sa edukasyon.
Courtesy to [**Olongapo City Information Center**](https://www.facebook.com/olongapopublicaffairs?__cft__[0]=AZUoEEuU7JRBp8m9YZ_lMtmfx9IJRxP901v3h3T0hfeZn1kxjLV0Z1r6Jfs3Bag4ifapazsLBegn1V1TQu1yB6pyZZx5ZTFUaZUlZqA2TOCdxP8_aqb4eGNYK49-vrS4sSl1XTuYTylL_EcYgARMMYYnCv7KUeZIwb2Y985F8OVk2jWSh389TouCTzffLa23nDTYIT4g6th1iQvxaJMV5SncNFYJ5OXzmrj-OwOukqkGvw&__tn__=-UC%2CP-y-R). Read full article [here](https://www.facebook.com/olongapopublicaffairs/posts/pfbid02Bss2CRG99Jb9rXr2nYMB6CQ7p5cWsYinmTc1Lh32eBMQ1V73JH1xNo6fxvSiRV7gl?__cft__[0]=AZUoEEuU7JRBp8m9YZ_lMtmfx9IJRxP901v3h3T0hfeZn1kxjLV0Z1r6Jfs3Bag4ifapazsLBegn1V1TQu1yB6pyZZx5ZTFUaZUlZqA2TOCdxP8_aqb4eGNYK49-vrS4sSl1XTuYTylL_EcYgARMMYYnCv7KUeZIwb2Y985F8OVk2jWSh389TouCTzffLa23nDTYIT4g6th1iQvxaJMV5SncNFYJ5OXzmrj-OwOukqkGvw&__tn__=%2CO%2CP-y-R).

# Reading Literacy Training
[Image: Reading Literacy Training]
[Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/extension/) [RDCES](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/rdces/) [Community Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/community-extension/)
**Saturday, June 10, 2023**
The GC CESU, Academic Affairs, and CEAS immersion instructors collaborated successfully to conduct a 3-day training for various CEAS academic programs. Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano played a pivotal role as the resource trainer, contributing to the training’s success. The training drew on the participation of a diverse group of student facilitators from ten programs within the College of Education, Arts, and Sciences. Specifically, 3rd-year students from BSEd (Mathematics, Science, English, Filipino, and Social Studies) and other programs like BPEd, BEEd, BCAEd, BECEd, and BAComm actively took part in the training. This training aligned with the goals of the MRP’s Reading Literacy Program for non-readers and out-of-school youth.

# 𝐎𝐒𝐖𝐒, 𝐏𝐄𝐒𝐎, 𝐃𝐎𝐋𝐄 𝐤𝐢𝐜𝐤 𝐨𝐟𝐟 𝐋𝐄𝐆𝐒 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟓
[Image: 𝐎𝐒𝐖𝐒, 𝐏𝐄𝐒𝐎, 𝐃𝐎𝐋𝐄 𝐤𝐢𝐜𝐤 𝐨𝐟𝐟 𝐋𝐄𝐆𝐒 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟓]
[OSWS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/osws/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, May 29, 2025**
The Office of Student Welfare and Services (OSWS) in partnership with the Department of Labor and Employment (DOLE) and Public Employment Service Office (PESO), conducted their annual Labor Education for Graduating Students (LEGS) 2025 yesterday, May 28, at the SMX Convention Center Olongapo.
The morning session included students from the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) and the College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) assisted by the Samahan ng mga Mag-aaral sa Araling Panlipunan (SMAP).
Meanwhile, the event continued until afternoon for the graduating students of College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS), College of Computer Studies (CCS), and College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS).
Started by Mr. Arvin Fabian, Senior Labor Employment Officer of DOLE, the seminar aims to equip its participants with sufficient knowledge regarding labor standards.
“Ang mandate po kasi ng DOLE is to ensure that everyone receive[s] what is due to them,” assured Mr. Fabian.
Additionally, this event was led by Ms. Jehan Doblado, Career Education and Job Placement Services Unit (CEJPSU) Coordinator, with the goal of preparing graduating studentsfor the corporate world.
“Para hindi sila zero knowledge. [Para] pagka-graduate nila alam nila ang gagawin nila,” the CEJPSU Coordinator said.
In her opening remarks, Ms. Lovelyn Ceralde, the Vice President of the Office of Student Welfare and Services (OSWS), stressed how necessary it is to study these standards.
“It is important that you, our future leaders and contributors, are well-versed in these fundamental principles,” said Ms. Ceralde.
She also highlighted the upcoming Commencement Exercises in July for the graduating batch 2025.
“See you on July 9 with your black togas!” the OSWS Vice President noted.
In an interview with The Forefront, some participants from different departments shared their insights on how the LEGS 2025 helped them as a graduating student of Gordon College.
“Ang laking tulong nitong seminar saken as a graduating student para maging prepared sa mga kakailanganin ko as a first time job seeker,” Jordan Magbanua, CCS Graduating Student claimed.
“Very helpful para sa mga graduating students lalo na sa akin kasi sa personal experience ko na naging employee na nag-resign para magpatuloy ang pag-aaral. Na-enlighten ako sa seminar na ito,” CEAS Graduating Student said.
Moreover, this event eyes recurrence every year in face-to-face set-up to ensure that graduating students remain well-informed regarding their rights as current or future employees.
[Image: ✍🏻]
: John Carlo B. Bermudez | Associate Editor
I-man Garcia | Circulation Manager
[Image: 📸]
: Bealyn Demanalata | Editor-in-Chief
Cindy Soriano | Photojournalist

# CCTV Privacy and Consent Notices
[Image: CCTV Privacy and Consent Notices]
[Alumni](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/alumni/) [LIMC](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/limc/)
**Monday, January 2, 2023**
To all stakeholders of Gordon College, please be informed that the Closed-Circuit Television (CCTV) cameras installed in the main building and premises of the college are used to process personal information, as defined by Republic Act 10173 or the Data Privacy act of 2012. These cameras capture and record the identities of individuals within their scope and are primarily intended for security and safety purposes. As mandated by the law, we would like to remind you that your consent is required for the processing of your personal information captured by these CCTV cameras. Your continued presence within the college premises is considered an implied consent to the processing of your personal information by the CCTV cameras. Rest assured that all personal information captured by the CCTV cameras will be treated with the utmost confidentiality and will only be accessed by authorized personnel for legitimate purposes. Thank you for your cooperation in ensuring compliance with the Data Privacy act of 2012.


### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-records-3127-freshmen-for-new-academic-year

# GC Records 3,127 Freshmen for New Academic Year
[Image: GC Records 3,127 Freshmen for New Academic Year]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Saturday, October 18, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE MAIN CAMPUS —Gordon College (GC) experiences an increase in its total student population for Academic Year 2025–2026, highlighted by a significant surge in freshmen enrollment across all college departments.
According to the official report from the Office of the College Registrar, the total student population rose from 8,625 this Academic Year (A.Y.) 2025-2026, in contrast to the 7,505 recorded Gcians last 2024–2025.
Furthermore, the number of Level 1 students also recorded a drastic jump from 2,731 to 3,127 freshmen.
The College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) continues to lead as the largest department, with 2,431 total students and 767 freshmen for this academic year.
The College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) recorded a population of 1,642 students and 676 freshmen.
The College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) also experienced a notable increase in freshmen enrollees, from 468 last year to 663 this year.
While the College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) saw an increase from 422 to 500 freshmen.
The College of Computer Studies (CCS) also recorded additional enrollees, welcoming 521 freshmen this year compared to the previous 442 record.
In line with its continuous growth, the CBA has officially moved into its new home at the GC Annex Building in Kalaklan, marking a new chapter for the college. | via Frances Marie Farne/The FOREFRONT

# ONLINE Adding/Dropping of Classes
[Image: ONLINE Adding/Dropping of Classes]
**Monday, January 19, 2026**
**ANNOUNCEMENT:**
**ONLINE Adding/Dropping of Classes**
*Second Semester Academic Year 2025-2026*
**January 19-30, 2026**
[Image: ☑️]
Step 1
Log-on to the student portal ([https://gordoncollegeccs.edu.ph/gces/student](https://gordoncollegeccs.edu.ph/gces/student?fbclid=IwZXh0bgNhZW0CMTAAYnJpZBExZWpzSlY3MHlQdVJWaXA0bnNydGMGYXBwX2lkEDIyMjAzOTE3ODgyMDA4OTIAAR5DCtJJZeE0IeMkAlOWYkAjlJ5HTa_uyuxL5M64lTq3dzJieHkPXJaQ0YalBg_aem_XfOZPmWrrx4p7jcYpDLqDg))
Click Add/Drop Request Button under Menu Items.
[Image: ☑️]
Step 2
To request for Dropping of classes, under Current Schedule section, click Drop button.
To request for adding of classes, under Add Subjects section, type the detail of the class (you can search by class code, subject or by description) on the search box. Click Add button of the desired class.
[Image: ☑️]
Step 3
Your selected subject(s) will appear under List of Subjects to Add/Drop section. Repeat step 2 to add or drop more classes.
Click submit to finalize your request or cancel to clear selected classes.
[Image: ☑️]
Step 4
Your request will be evaluated by your program coordinator and will be verified by the college registrar.
Regularly visit the GCES portal for request updates.

# Gordon College Launches State-of-the-Art Mac Laboratory
[Image: Gordon College Launches State-of-the-Art Mac Laboratory]
[CCS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ccs/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, April 28, 2025**
Gordon College (GC) proudly announces the official launch of its new Mac Laboratory, a significant enhancement to the technological resources available to its students. The laboratory is equipped with 25 advanced iMac M4 units, representing a substantial investment in providing students with industry-standard technology to support their academic pursuits and skill development.
GC extends its deepest gratitude to the City Mayor of Olongapo, the Honorable Rolen C. Paulino Jr., for his steadfast commitment to the betterment of the College and his invaluable support for this initiative.
“The launch of the Mac Laboratory is a momentous occasion for Gordon College, made possible through the collective vision and support of our esteemed City Mayor, dedicated officials, and valued stakeholders,” stated Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, College President. “These state-of-the-art iMac M4 units will empower our students with the technological proficiency demanded by today’s industries, further solidifying our commitment to providing a world-class education.”
This initiative was also made possible through the strong support of Hon. Cristabelle Marie C. Paulino, Chairperson of the Committee on Education, Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, College President, Dr. Erlinda S. Casela-Abarintos, Dean of the College of Computer Studies and the Vice President for Administration and Finance, Dr. Darwin P. Paguio, Vice President for Academic Affairs, College Officials, and other stakeholders. This advancement underscores GC’s commitment to providing cutting-edge resources for its students.
Together, we will strive for more innovations for the college, empowering our students and faculty to excel in the ever-evolving landscape of technology and design.

# CLEARANCE REQUIREMENTS FOR NON-GRADUATING STUDENTS
[Image: CLEARANCE REQUIREMENTS FOR NON-GRADUATING STUDENTS]
**Tuesday, May 21, 2024**
𝘼𝙩𝙩𝙚𝙣𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣, 𝙣𝙤𝙣-𝙜𝙧𝙖𝙙𝙪𝙖𝙩𝙞𝙣𝙜 𝙨𝙩𝙪𝙙𝙚𝙣𝙩𝙨!
Clearance checklist:
[Image: ☑️]
Students must be cleared of any pending Disciplinary Work from the Student Discipline Unit.
[Image: ☑️]
Students have complied with the required Medical Examination for Academic Year 2023-2024.
[Image: ☑️]
Students must answer the SWS Satisfaction Survey: [bit.ly/SWSSurvey2023](https://bit.ly/SWSSurvey2023?fbclid=IwZXh0bgNhZW0CMTAAAR02rWq9j-UvPUVSD0d7EuErFJ0VxCOpOWbuoWqxp9lxrD9gpbUpXZp9170_aem_Ac3e0VysXi3cg6UlqneI4sem80M42MlW8A1OQjXgXh_ePcCgehwfW8nkGxxZxF5yVV2HXRFvzVPek33i2qkBjZ-8)
𝗡𝗢𝗧𝗘: Students who will enroll this midyear term must complete the survey on or before June 3, 2024
[Image: ☑️]
Student Organization Officers must be free from any obligation from their organization and have complied with and submitted the required documents/reports by the Student Organization Unit

# Institutional Brigada Eskwela 2023
[Image: Institutional Brigada Eskwela 2023]
[Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/extension/) [RDCES](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/rdces/) [Community Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/community-extension/)
**Thursday, August 17, 2023**
Brigada Eskwela served as a prime exemplar of how collaborative efforts across different sectors can enhance education in the Philippines. This program effectively united a diverse array of stakeholders to advance DepEd’s mission of ensuring equitable access to quality education for all Filipino learners.
In partnership with GC Student Organizations, the GC Community Extension Services Unit (GC-CESU) and the Office of Student Welfare and Services (OSWS) demonstrated unwavering dedication to providing vital community services. A significant objective of the institution was to create a conducive learning environment for students. Consequently, the GC community wholeheartedly participated in Brigada Eskwela 2023 (Brigada Clean-Up Drive), which yielded remarkable outcomes. Numerous Gordon College student organizations and academic staff from various programs actively contributed to this noble undertaking.

# 𝘼 𝙇𝙚𝙜𝙖𝙘𝙮 𝙤𝙛 𝙀𝙭𝙘𝙚𝙡𝙡𝙚𝙣𝙘𝙚, 𝘼 𝙁𝙪𝙩𝙪𝙧𝙚 𝙤𝙛 𝙄𝙢𝙥𝙖𝙘𝙩
[Image: 𝘼 𝙇𝙚𝙜𝙖𝙘𝙮 𝙤𝙛 𝙀𝙭𝙘𝙚𝙡𝙡𝙚𝙣𝙘𝙚, 𝘼 𝙁𝙪𝙩𝙪𝙧𝙚 𝙤𝙛 𝙄𝙢𝙥𝙖𝙘𝙩]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, August 17, 2025**
Gordon College is proud to announce that the Philippine Society of Customs Administration Students (PSCAS) Gordon College Chapter soared to new heights at the PSCAS National Awards on August 16, 2025, at Wind Residences, Tagaytay City!
PSCAS GC Chapter was honored with the most prestigious award: Top Performing PSCAS Chapter!
This incredible achievement is a testament to our dedication to excellence and unwavering commitment to our community.
We also brought home a constellation of awards, including Most Outstanding in:
\* Community Impact & Civic Engagement
\* Leadership & Institutional Development Projects
\* Specialized Customs Training & Webinar Programs
And we were recognized as Outstanding in:
\* Academic Excellence & Professional Development
\* Career Preparation & Skills Development
\* Culture, Arts & Wellness Promotion
\* Environmental Advocacy & Sustainability
\* Student Engagement & Community Building Initiatives
\* Student Support & Mentorship Initiatives
We want to give a special shout-out to two of our very own who stood out among the best students in the country:
\* Kris Rean Agaton was named the Top 1 Most Outstanding BSCA Student of the Philippines 2025.
\* Renmmelle Kyle Cerilles was named among the top 3 most outstanding BSCA Students in the Philippines in 2025.
Your hard work, leadership, and passion have made a mark, and we couldn’t be prouder of your achievements.
This incredible success belongs to every member, officer, and adviser who contributed their time and talent to make our chapter shine.
Thank you for your tireless efforts!

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/gc-marks-second-year-in-one-billion-rising-movement-to-end-violence-against-women-and-girls

# GC marks second year in One Billion Rising movement to end violence against women and girls
[Image: GC marks second year in One Billion Rising movement to end violence against women and girls]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, February 16, 2026**
Gordon College marked its second consecutive year of participation in the global campaign One Billion Rising through a public awareness activity themed “Rise for our bodies, our Earth, our Future,” held at Ayala Malls Harbor Point on February 14.  
The event brought together students and mall-goers in a collective call to stand against violence toward women and girls while promoting empowerment and social responsibility.  
Established in 2012, One Billion Rising has developed into an international movement recognized for mobilizing communities across the world to advocate for the elimination of gender-based violence.  
The initiative is grounded in global data from the United Nations indicating that approximately one in three women experience violence at some point in their lives, representing more than a billion women and girls worldwide.  
The activity was led by the Institutional Planning, Development, and External Affairs (IPDEA) through the Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) Unit in partnership with the National Service Training Program (NSTP) and Ayala Malls Inc., with the goal of strengthening public awareness and encouraging community participation in the advocacy.  
One of the program’s highlights was a flash mob performance by Pase Ballo and SHAPES, who led a mass dance to the One Billion Rising anthem, Break the Chain, symbolizing unity and resistance.  
Mr. Joseph Atencio, head of the DEI Unit, emphasized that violence against women and girls does not only occur in public spaces but is often present within homes and personal relationships.  
“Bukod sa awareness na gusto nating ipakalat, gusto nating maging mapagmatyag ‘yung mga tao. The purpose of One Billion Rising is truly to end violence against women and girls. There can be no sustainable development without women’s empowerment,” he remarked. | via Alyssa Marie Alcantara/The FOREFRONT
Photos by Ellisha Dawn & Angela Samson
[#InformingandEmpowering](https://www.facebook.com/hashtag/informingandempowering?__eep__=6&__cft__[0]=AZaJoTDLG9gJnuDWS-RIuI8XwO1QiUv2FTKmy-U5r0QFiLAEfnc3NNmeYXgIwqBqezEW-cjTa11AipgJttjyDV1PJHIZkXbdgREWOo33V9JvhvFjIq3JYaMRHmTgUCxs5OuQn3saZIN1CVbnurMj_lEK6u7PC90-6t9i07Q4kqwTGJyE-d3CMRi4WZQC0vefVdk&__tn__=*NK-R)

# 𝐌𝐗. 𝐆𝐂 𝐒𝐏𝐄𝐂𝐈𝐀𝐋 𝐀𝐖𝐀𝐑𝐃𝐒 𝐇𝐈𝐆𝐋𝐈𝐆𝐇𝐓𝐒
[Image: 𝐌𝐗. 𝐆𝐂 𝐒𝐏𝐄𝐂𝐈𝐀𝐋 𝐀𝐖𝐀𝐑𝐃𝐒 𝐇𝐈𝐆𝐋𝐈𝐆𝐇𝐓𝐒]
[OSWS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/osws/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, February 20, 2024**
GORDON COLLEGE — As the competition arises, five- contestants received a triumphant win to special awards.
On a two-sets of awarding, Mx. Albert Panotes, Mx. College of Health and Allied Studies (CAHS) received the Mx. Congeniality, Mx. Beautiful Body, Best in Pride Attire, and Best in Formal Wear.
Meanwhile, Vench Paje, Mx. College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS), garnered the Best Advocacy Video and Best Casual Wear.
Furthermore, Josh Allen Bermudez, the Mx. College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) hailed as the Mx. Photogenic, Social Media Award, Mx. People’s Choice, and Best Talent.
And to end the night, Joshua Salgado, Mx. College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) is awarded with Mx. Healthy Smile and Mx. Perfect Skin.
The contestants received a sash and certificate fully-proving that the title is theirs anymore.

# Colegio de Masinloc Visits Gordon College for Benchmarking Toward Institutional Recognition
[Image: Colegio de Masinloc Visits Gordon College for Benchmarking Toward Institutional Recognition]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, August 29, 2025**
On August 27, 2025, Gordon College had the privilege of hosting Colegio de Masinloc for a benchmarking visit as they gear up for their application for Institutional Recognition, a crucial step toward qualifying for the Free Higher Education program under the Universal Access to Quality Tertiary Education Act (Republic Act 10931).
Their delegation, headed by Dr. Feliciano M. Rosete Jr. – College President, explored Gordon College’s best practices and gathered insights on CHED’s requirements for Institutional Capacity, Program Offerings, and Physical Facilities. The visit also aligned with their plans to expand academic offerings with new programs in Computer Science, Entrepreneurship, Accountancy, and Civil Engineering.
Gordon College is honored to share its experiences and extend its support to partner institutions in advancing the quality and accessibility of higher education.
[#SDG4QualityEducation](https://www.facebook.com/hashtag/sdg4qualityeducation?__eep__=6&__cft__[0]=AZWF2kWWz8Z1Pbw_oREe1MAEnmt71ZKdhnGeg2bH-dFMehssIwQ0hYuHOy32bEQ2mOXtAOLrYIolfh-gkEJx7cIey8oMLcxjHxK7Ipcirt9rz-wf4-_ZaawmD5rj1EOU_dpz4IJBuwEsmFR4IWjw8wbgJtH_W17J79F_lpb-upGmaUoJamnNAcasTaaWKTOBkq6OMU70CI4Or9n-qf1UDH_NkCmOPusaGbQO-bd7gB-Z7g&__tn__=*NK-y-R)
[#SDG17partnershipsforthegoals](https://www.facebook.com/hashtag/sdg17partnershipsforthegoals?__eep__=6&__cft__[0]=AZWF2kWWz8Z1Pbw_oREe1MAEnmt71ZKdhnGeg2bH-dFMehssIwQ0hYuHOy32bEQ2mOXtAOLrYIolfh-gkEJx7cIey8oMLcxjHxK7Ipcirt9rz-wf4-_ZaawmD5rj1EOU_dpz4IJBuwEsmFR4IWjw8wbgJtH_W17J79F_lpb-upGmaUoJamnNAcasTaaWKTOBkq6OMU70CI4Or9n-qf1UDH_NkCmOPusaGbQO-bd7gB-Z7g&__tn__=*NK-y-R)

# Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Awareness Campaign
[Image: Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Awareness Campaign]
[Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/extension/) [RDCES](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/rdces/) [Community Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/community-extension/)
**Monday, March 13, 2023**
The successful execution of earthquake and fire prevention simulation exercises is essential for reducing risks and enhancing disaster preparation. These simulations play an essential role in strengthening the resilience of communities and organisations in the face of earthquakes and fires. These sessions were successfully carried out owing to the committed efforts of CESU coordinators, admin/faculty volunteers as facilitators, and our resource speakers, Ms. Jerica Baccay (Head of GC DRRMU) and Mr. Jun Ecleo (GC Safety Officer).

[Image: GCAAI Donated 120 Chairs]

# GC Library Renovation: 40% Done
[Image: GC Library Renovation: 40% Done]
[LIMC](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/limc/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, August 19, 2024**
The Gordon College library is currently undergoing renovation project which aims at transforming the space into a more dynamic and student-friendly environment.
In an interview with The Forefront, Mr. Marlon Atencio, the college librarian, provided detailed updates and valuable insights into the objectives, current developments, and anticipated benefits of the project for students and faculty alike.
According to Mr. Atencio, the upgraded library is shifting towards a “learning commons” setup.
“Kasi yung college library natin ang mangyayari ay parang learning commons na siya, kumbaga parang café setup,” he shared.
In addition, the college librarian announced plans to enhance the library by installing vending machines, similar to those at ground floor, allowing students to enjoy snacks—though only solid foods, other snacks, will be permitted, with liquids excluded.
He further mentioned that the timeline for the renovation’s completion remains flexible.
“Wala naman binigay na timeline, ang gusto lang manyari is as soon as possible matapos siya para siyempre magamit na ito ng mga estudyante.” he added
As of now, the renovation is around 40% complete. Key areas such as the computer section and audiovisual room are being redesigned with high-quality materials.
Moreover, he also shared that various areas, such as the flooring are already underway and the renovation is focusing on upgrading both the structural and aesthetic aspects of the library to ensure a premium experience for GCians.
Students and faculty can look forward to an enhanced library experience. The space are expected to be more conducive to learning with upgraded seating, including sofas and modern furniture.
Furthermore, the college librarian also mentioned that they should not only expect the library to be well-maintained but also that its services will be improved to ensure that students can use it effectively.
In addressing the specific needs of students and faculty, the library will host separate orientation sessions to familiarize students with the new setup.
“Kaya hindi kami nakisabay sa student orientation kasi magkakaroon kami ng separate orientation sa library, wherein ang gagawin namin ay mag-kakaroon ng library tour ang bawat section, para alam nila kung paano gamitin lahat ng area, para maiwasan din natin [na] nahihirapan kung paano i-access [ang] mga gamit, pati mga electronics resources lahat ‘yon it-tackle natin during orientation,” he explains.
Next, he also discussed changes in the physical layout, such as new glass doors and relocating the lockers outside the library.
Additionally, he encouraged students to reach out through the library’s Facebook page for online inquiries.
Once completed, the renovated Gordon College library promises to be a welcoming and efficient learning space for all that fosters both learning and collaboration.

# EASE ON SCHOOL UNIFORMPOLICY DUE TO EXTREMEHEAT CONDITIONS (EXTENSION)
[Image: EASE ON SCHOOL UNIFORMPOLICY DUE TO EXTREMEHEAT CONDITIONS (EXTENSION)]
[OSWS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/osws/)
**Saturday, May 4, 2024**
𝙀𝙮𝙚𝙨 𝙝𝙚𝙧𝙚, 𝙂𝘾𝙞𝙖𝙣𝙨!
Due to the expected hot and humid weather conditions, the relaxation of the uniform policy will continue to be in effect throughout the month of May.
𝙁𝙧𝙤𝙢 𝙈𝙤𝙣𝙙𝙖𝙮𝙨 𝙩𝙤 𝙏𝙝𝙪𝙧𝙨𝙙𝙖𝙮𝙨, students will have the option to wear civilian attire, 𝙬𝙝𝙞𝙡𝙚 𝙨𝙩𝙞𝙡𝙡 𝙛𝙤𝙡𝙡𝙤𝙬𝙞𝙣𝙜 𝙩𝙝𝙚 𝙙𝙧𝙚𝙨𝙨 𝙘𝙤𝙙𝙚 𝙥𝙤𝙡𝙞𝙘𝙮.
Refer to <https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/announcement/2050/> for more information.

# Final presentation of Project METRO
[Image: Final presentation of Project METRO]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, April 17, 2026**
April 16, 2026 | Final presentation of Project METRO: Monitoring Enforcement Tracking Resource Online with DTI Zambales and Gordon College – College of Computer Studies.
Present during the final presentation are DTI3 CPD Acting Division Chief Jose Antonio Toledo, DTI Zambales CPD Division Chief Neil John M. Fabay, Gordon College CCS Dean Ronnie D. Luy, Gordon College CCS Asst Dean Armilyn T. Martinez, and Gordon College CCS Prof. Reynaldo G. Bautista. The panelist provided expert guidance and valuable insights to further refining the system.
Project METRO is intended to make market inspections and fair trade monitoring more efficient, transparent, and fully digitized. In practical terms, the system aims to help government personnel carry out inspections more systematically while improving documentation, reporting, and coordination in the field.
The use of a digital platform for inspections will also help strengthen public trust by making enforcement more transparent and easier to document. In many cases, digitized systems can support more consistent monitoring while reducing gaps in manual processes.
[DTI Region 3 – Central Luzon](https://www.facebook.com/DTI.REG3?__cft__[0]=AZYxLDsjdTzjPYOLTvNuRJP61gJUnjHgJYtSaDA56opcLQXJdlVEec6z26I38rhYOQMlmQWbiGi3cSN-BI8rFLDqIJ8N8x0letYLtJQLdW8isvXqMFeFhGEZjscuRXMgk1IOms9Ej52Q1DcRgIlyuXFi&__tn__=-]K-R)[Gordon College](https://www.facebook.com/GordonCollegeOfficial?__cft__[0]=AZYxLDsjdTzjPYOLTvNuRJP61gJUnjHgJYtSaDA56opcLQXJdlVEec6z26I38rhYOQMlmQWbiGi3cSN-BI8rFLDqIJ8N8x0letYLtJQLdW8isvXqMFeFhGEZjscuRXMgk1IOms9Ej52Q1DcRgIlyuXFi&__tn__=-]K-R)

# GC CEAS Mentors Declared Champions of KOLEHIYAW: Interschool Debate Competition 2025
[Image: GC CEAS Mentors Declared Champions of KOLEHIYAW: Interschool Debate Competition 2025]
[CEAS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ceas/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, April 25, 2025**
“𝘛𝘰 𝘣𝘦 𝘢 𝘤𝘩𝘢𝘮𝘱𝘪𝘰𝘯, 𝙘𝙤𝙢𝙥𝙚𝙩𝙚; 𝘵𝘰 𝘣𝘦 𝘢 𝘨𝘳𝘦𝘢𝘵 𝘤𝘩𝘢𝘮𝘱𝘪𝘰𝘯, 𝙘𝙤𝙢𝙥𝙚𝙩𝙚 𝙬𝙞𝙩𝙝 𝙩𝙝𝙚 𝙗𝙚𝙨𝙩; 𝒃𝒖𝒕 𝒕𝒐 𝒃𝒆 𝒕𝒉𝒆 𝒈𝒓𝒆𝒂𝒕𝒆𝒔𝒕 𝒄𝒉𝒂𝒎𝒑𝒊𝒐𝒏, 𝙘𝙤𝙢𝙥𝙚𝙩𝙚 𝙬𝙞𝙩𝙝 𝙮𝙤𝙪𝙧𝙨𝙚𝙡𝙛.”
– 𝐌𝐚𝐭𝐬𝐡𝐨𝐧𝐚 𝐃𝐡𝐥𝐢𝐰𝐚𝐲𝐨
𝗖𝗼𝗻𝗴𝗿𝗮𝘁𝘀 to our College of Education, Arts, and Sciences student representative—𝗠𝗿. 𝗝𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗵𝘀𝗼𝗻 𝗔. 𝗠𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗼𝗿𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗮𝘀, 𝗟𝘂𝗰𝗸𝘆 𝗨𝗲𝗹 𝗔. 𝗠𝗮𝗴𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗮, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗠𝘀. 𝗟𝗼𝘃𝗲 𝗟𝗼𝗿𝗮𝗶𝗻𝗻𝗲 𝗩𝗲𝗻𝘂𝘀 𝗣. 𝗔𝗰𝗲𝗱𝗼, as well as their coaches 𝗠𝘀. 𝗝𝗲𝗵𝗮𝗻 𝗗𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗮𝗱𝗼 and 𝗠𝗿. 𝗖𝗵𝗿𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗮𝗻 𝗟𝗼𝗶𝗱 𝗩𝗮𝗹𝗲𝗻𝘇𝘂𝗲𝗹𝗮, for being 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗺𝗽𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝗻 𝗞𝗢𝗟𝗘𝗛𝗜𝗬𝗔𝗪 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿-𝘀𝗰𝗵𝗼𝗼𝗹 𝗗𝗲𝗯𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗲𝘁𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟱 𝗮𝘁 𝗖𝗼𝗹𝘂𝗺𝗯𝗮𝗻 𝗖𝗼𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗴𝗲.
They have also won and received the following awards:
2𝒏𝒅 𝑩𝒆𝒔𝒕 𝑫𝒆𝒃𝒂𝒕𝒆𝒓 — 𝐋𝐨𝐯𝐞 𝐋𝐨𝐫𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐧𝐞 𝐕𝐞𝐧𝐮𝐬 𝐏. 𝐀𝐜𝐞𝐝𝐨
𝑷𝒆𝒐𝒑𝒍𝒆’𝒔 𝑪𝒉𝒐𝒊𝒄𝒆 𝑨𝒘𝒂𝒓𝒅 — 𝐋𝐮𝐜𝐤𝐲 𝐔𝐞𝐥 𝐌𝐚𝐠𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐚
𝘖𝘯 𝘣𝘦𝘩𝘢𝘭𝘧 𝘰𝘧 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘊𝘌𝘈𝘚 𝘧𝘢𝘮𝘪𝘭𝘺, 𝙬𝙚 𝙖𝙧𝙚 𝙖𝙡𝙡 𝙨𝙤 𝙥𝙧𝙤𝙪𝙙 𝙤𝙛 𝙮𝙤𝙪. Baka 𝗧𝗔𝗧𝗔𝗞 𝗚𝗖𝗶𝗮𝗻𝘀 𝘆𝗮𝗻!

# Strengthening Research Excellence: Instructors Oriented on IPR and Ethical Practices
[Image: Strengthening Research Excellence: Instructors Oriented on IPR and Ethical Practices]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, April 6, 2026**
The Research Instructors’ Orientation, Upskilling, and Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) Seminar Series, held on March 31, 2026, at the Cosme Y. Sering AVR, was successfully conducted with the active participation of faculty members and research instructors.
The seminar served as a valuable platform to reorient and enhance participants’ knowledge and competencies in key research processes, particularly in research ethics, research processes, intellectual property rights (IPR), and Research Integrity. Meaningful discussions and insights were shared, reinforcing the importance of ethical and responsible research practices within the academic community.
The activity was facilitated by Dr. Ediric D. Gadia (VP, RDCES) and Ms. Nicole Suero (Coordinator, PU and IPMU), whose expertise greatly contributed to the seminar’s success.
This initiative reflects the institution’s continued commitment to strengthening research capability and fostering a culture of excellence and integrity among its educators.  
CTTO: **[Gordon College-Rdces](https://www.facebook.com/gc.rdces.7/posts/pfbid02deeYC1CN9webEa4V4nWyUcws3RVaZnUBDoteFqZCi58W7XfmuGTM78AKNWH7ZY2zl?__cft__[0]=AZZYyoux5wCl2efMtLfj70IRRxSIX60Zdagknk8fvhDpEQGaySvaaNgrZ8wEXWE1ihYa_GXTEiGzHjYsHRpXK3dxKI_LPD0YgOVOvE4tB3EjpbCSbpABZLuqky-nn-AJx7ilMC0qQg3qLI1EngcdQZN8NvJf8Gi8env6rGBXMWtTHfYMJ5m4u5-bENvvDpKJhKnVwQiEkOruW7Hi3PigFqNK7NpW3usxP6t8v84i07Vb7g&__tn__=%2CO%2CP-y-R)**

# Rights of the Data Subjects
The stakeholders are the primary data subjects of Gordon College. They are the students, their parents or guardians, the employees (regardless of their employment status), officials, visitors, donors/ sponsors, service providers, and the like are entitled to the following rights with regards to their personal data:
[Image: 300px\_300px\_rights-informed-01]
## Rights to be Informed
* The data subject has a right to be informed whether personal data pertaining to him or her shall be, are being, or have been processed, including the existence of automated decision-making and profiling.
* The data subject shall be notified and furnished with information indicated hereunder before the entry of his or her personal data into the processing system of Gordon College, or at the next practical opportunity:
  + Description of the personal data to be entered into the system;
  + Purposes for which they are being or will be processed, including processing for direct marketing, profiling or historical, statistical or scientific purpose;
  + Basis of processing, when processing is not based on the consent of the data subject;
  + Scope and method of the personal data processing;
  + Third-party recipients or classes of recipients to whom the personal data are or may be disclosed;
  + Methods utilized for automated access, if the same is allowed by the data subject, and the extent to which such access is authorized, including meaningful information about the logic involved, as well as the significance and the envisaged consequences of such processing for the data subject;
  + The identity and contact details of Gordon College or its representative;
  + The period for which the information will be stored; and
  + The existence of their rights as data subjects, including the right to access, correction, and object to the processing, as well as the right to lodge a complaint before the National Privacy Commission.
For more information you may refer to <https://privacy.gov.ph/the-right-to-be-informed/>
[Image: 300px\_300px\_object-02]
## Right to Object
The data subject shall have the right to object to the processing of his or her personal data, including processing for direct marketing, automated processing or profiling. The data subject shall also be notified and given an opportunity to withhold consent to the processing in case of changes or any amendment to the information supplied or declared to the data subject in the preceding paragraph.  
When a data subject objects or withholds consent, Gordon College may not be able to conduct academic, administrative and other functions or services related to the data subject.  
When a data subject objects or withholds consent, Gordon College shall no longer process the personal data, unless:
* The personal data is needed pursuant to a subpoena;
* The collection and processing are for obvious purposes, including, when it is necessary for the performance of or in relation to a contract or service to which the data subject is a party, or when necessary or desirable in the context of an employer-employee relationship between the collector and the data subject;
* The information is being collected and processed as a result of a legal obligation; or
* Gordon College’s functions as the local college or as an instrumentality of the government will be impaired.
For more information you may refer to <https://privacy.gov.ph/right-to-object/>
[Image: 300px\_300px\_rights-access-03]
## Right to Access
The data subject has the right to reasonable access to, upon demand providing sufficient time for preparation, the following:
* Contents of his or her personal data that were processed;
* Sources from which personal data were obtained;
* Names and addresses of recipients of the personal data;
* Manner by which such data were processed;
* Reasons for the disclosure of the personal data to recipients, if any;
* Information on automated processes where the data will, or is likely to, be made as the sole basis for any decision that significantly affects or will affect the data subject;
  + Date when his or her personal data concerning the data subject were last accessed and modified; and
  + The description of Gordon College under Olongapo City Ordinance 07 Series of 2018.
For more information you may refer to <https://privacy.gov.ph/right-to-access/>
[Image: 300px\_300px\_rights-correct-04]
## Right to Correct/ Rectification
The data subject has the right to dispute the inaccuracy or error in the personal data and have Gordon College correct it within a reasonable period and accordingly, unless the request is vexatious or otherwise unreasonable. If the personal data has been corrected, Gordon College shall ensure the accessibility of both the new and the retracted information and the simultaneous receipt of the new and the retracted information by the intended recipients thereof: provided, that recipients or third parties who have previously received such processed personal data shall be informed of its inaccuracy and its rectification, upon reasonable request of the Data Subject.
For more information you may refer to <https://privacy.gov.ph/right-to-rectify/>
[Image: 300px\_300px\_rights-eraser-05]
## Right to Block/ Remove/ Erasure/ Be Forgotten
These rights of erasure and blocking do not apply to personal data, documents, records and accounts which are part of Gordon College public records as an instrumentality of the government or as the local college.
The data subject shall have the right to suspend, withdraw or order the blocking, removal or destruction of his or her personal data from Gordon College’s filing system.
* This right may be exercised upon discovery and substantial proof of any of the following:
  + The personal data is incomplete, outdated, false, or unlawfully obtained;
  + The personal data is being used for purpose not authorized by the  
    data subject;
  + The personal data is no longer necessary for the purposes for which  
    they were collected;
  + The data subject withdraws consent or objects to the processing, and there is no other legal ground or overriding legitimate interest for the processing;
  + The processing is unlawful;
  + Gordon College personal information processor violated the rights of the data subject.
* Gordon College may notify third parties who have previously received such  
  processed personal data.
For more information you may refer to <https://privacy.gov.ph/right-to-erasure-or-blocking/>
[Image: 300px\_300px\_rights-complaint-06]
## Right to File a Complaint
You have the right to lodge a complaint if you are the subject of a data privacy violation.
For more information you may refer to <https://privacy.gov.ph/right-to-file-a-complaint/>
[Image: 300px\_300px\_rights-damages-09]
## Right to be Indemnified/ to Damages
This right to damages is subordinate to:
* The presumption of Gordon College regularity in the performance of government functions;
* As applicable, the principle that the State may not be sued without its consent; and
* The non-liability of Gordon College arising from the incidental damages due to Gordon College’s pursuance of its mandates or compliance with its legal obligations.
The data subject shall be indemnified for any damages sustained due to such inaccurate, incomplete, outdated, false, unlawfully obtained or unauthorized use of personal data, taking into account any violation of his or her rights and freedoms as data subject.
For more information you may refer to <https://privacy.gov.ph/right-to-damages/>
[Image: 300px\_300px\_rights-portability-08]
## Right to Portability
As a data subject, you have the right to obtain from the PIC a copy of your personal data and/or have the same transmitted from one PIC to another, in an electronic or structured format that is commonly used.
For more information you may refer to <https://privacy.gov.ph/right-to-data-portability/>

  [Right to Access](#rightaccess)

  [Right to File a Complaint](#rightcomplaint)  
  [Right to Damages](#rightdamages)  
  [Right to Portability](#rightportable)

# 𝐈𝐬𝐚𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐠𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐢𝐛𝐨 𝐚𝐭 𝐈𝐧𝐤𝐥𝐮𝐬𝐢𝐛𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐚𝐦𝐮𝐦𝐮𝐧𝐨, 𝐛𝐢𝐧𝐢𝐠𝐲𝐚𝐧 𝐧𝐠 𝐛𝐚𝐠𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐩𝐞𝐤𝐭𝐢𝐛𝐨
[Image: 𝐈𝐬𝐚𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐠𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐢𝐛𝐨 𝐚𝐭 𝐈𝐧𝐤𝐥𝐮𝐬𝐢𝐛𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐚𝐦𝐮𝐦𝐮𝐧𝐨, 𝐛𝐢𝐧𝐢𝐠𝐲𝐚𝐧 𝐧𝐠 𝐛𝐚𝐠𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐩𝐞𝐤𝐭𝐢𝐛𝐨]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, July 18, 2025**
Pinaglaanan ng panibagong pananaw ang mga kasalukuyang student leader ng Dalubhasaan Gordon patungkol sa paksa ng pamumuno bilang lider sa katatapos na Capacity Building and Personality Development Program for Student Organizations 2025 na ginanap sa Function 1, SMX Olongapo City Convention Center.
Ang naturang seminar na may temang: “Leadership Rooted in Purpose: Strengthening Self, Serving Others, Shaping Tomorrow” ay dinaluhan ng dalawang resource speaker mula Central Luzon State University (CLSU) na sina Dr. Irene G. Bustos, CLSU Student Affairs and Services Dean at ni Ms. Joan B. Pelagio, CLSU Paruparong Bukid Adviser.
Ang nabanggit na talakayan ay pinasimulan ni Dr. Bustos sa kanyang presentasyon tungkol sa kung paano nga ba ang tamang pamumuno ng isang lider sa kanyang nasasakupan at mga kalidad na dapat niyang tinataglay. Binigyang pansin din ni Dr. Bustos kung paano gawing reyalidad ang bisyon ng isang lider.
Sa kabilang banda, binuksan naman ni Ms. Joan B. Pelagio, CLSU Paruparong Bukid Adviser, ang diskusyong hinggil sa pagiging gender-responsive at pagbuo ng isang inklusibong kapaligiran at sa loob ng isang institusyon.
Ayon kay Ms. Pelagio, sa pamamagitan ng gender-responsiveness at inklusibong pakikitungo, mas makakabuo tayo ng pagkakaisa, respeto, at ligtas kapaligiran.
“Nag-start kami with few so with support and respect na hindi kami hinahayaan kung hindi sinusuportahan kami na gawin kung ano yung mga activities na kailangan, ‘yun yung isa sa nagmo-motivate sa group o sa org para ituloy yung advocacy.” Saad ni Ms. Pelagio ukol sa lagay ng Paruparong Bukid sa kanilang institusyon.
Si Ms. Pelagio ang tagapayo ng Paruparong Bukid, isang organisasyong nabuo sa isang obserbasyon at pagsasaliksik nina Dr. Bustos patungkol sa homophobia at transphobia na siyang naging pasimundan ng MAYARI na organisayon naman ng LGBTQIA+ ng Dalubhasaang Gordon.
Sa kabuuan ng nasabing seminar, “If you have passion for change, keep that passion fired, ika nga, [and] lighted, and pursue your vision and make it a reality. Be aware of everything that happens within and outside yourself. And kung anuman ang mga challenges, mga issues, problems ‘no parte lahat ito ng pagiging leader kaya hold on and keep the fire burning — yung passion mo to lead and to make change for the betterment of your studentry, the community, and siyempre the nation as a whole.” Ani Dr. Bustos sa mga kasalukuyang student leader at nagnanais na maging lider sa hinaharap.

# 𝐏𝐚𝐠𝐥𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐩𝐚𝐭 𝐧𝐠 𝐤𝐚𝐩𝐚𝐧𝐠𝐲𝐚𝐫𝐢𝐡𝐚𝐧 𝐬𝐚 𝐛𝐚𝐠𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐦𝐢𝐧𝐨, 𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐥 𝐧𝐚𝐧𝐠 𝐧𝐚𝐢𝐬𝐚𝐤𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐚𝐧
[Image: 𝐏𝐚𝐠𝐥𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐩𝐚𝐭 𝐧𝐠 𝐤𝐚𝐩𝐚𝐧𝐠𝐲𝐚𝐫𝐢𝐡𝐚𝐧 𝐬𝐚 𝐛𝐚𝐠𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐦𝐢𝐧𝐨, 𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐥 𝐧𝐚𝐧𝐠 𝐧𝐚𝐢𝐬𝐚𝐤𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐚𝐧]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, July 18, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE — Isinalin na ang mga posisyon at kapangyarihan sa mga bagong opisyales, gayundin ang pagpapakilala sa mga bagong kakatawan sa mga GCians sa katatapos na Supreme Student Council Turnover Ceremony na ginanap sa PE Hall nitong ika-14 ng Hulyo.
Nasaksihan din ng mga lider-estudyante mula sa iba’t ibang organisasyon sa loob ng institusyon ang nabanggit na seremonya.
Bilang panimula, binigyang diin ng noo’y SSC Chairperson Frances Daphne Mobo ang seremonya sa paglalahad ng Budget at Accomplishment Reports ng kanyang termino.
Samantala, inamin ni Mobo na nabigo nilang tuparin ang ilang mga pangakong proyekto para sa mga mag-aaral.
“Failures make us better individuals. Leadership teaches you how to be accountable [for] your own actions and how to accept constructive criticism. To our student leaders present here today, do remember that leadership is not about positions or titles because those are temporary. Leadership is about passion and dedication,” aniya.
Bukod dito, binigyan ng pagkakataon ang bagong termino upang talakayin at bigyang linaw ang kanilang mga inihandang proyekto at aktibidades sa harap ng kanilang mga kapwa lider-estudyante.
Sumatutal, mayroong 12 na proyekto at 17 na aktibidades na gustong ilunsad ang naturang termino sa pangunguna ni ngayo’y SSC Chairperson Julier Samonte. Ilan dito ay ang GCLAMP 2.0: Enhancing Access in the GCLAMP, CODE 200: Tech Industry Partnership Found, BIN-novation: Classroom for a Cleaner Future para sa kanilang proyekto, samantala ay inahain naman ang Student First Starter Packs: College Life Unboxed, Think Green: Green Revolutions for GCians, Beyond the Myths: Understanding HIV/AIDS & Prevention para sa kanilang mga aktibidades.
Ngunit ang lahat ng kanilang programa ay pawang mga panukala pa lamang at posibleng mayroong ibang hindi mapayagang mailunsad, paglilinaw ng bagong konseho.
Bilang karagdagan sa mga pangako ni Samonte, bagong kinatawan ng konseho, nais niya at ng kaniyang konseho na palawakin ang komunikasyon at transparency sa pagsasagawa ng regular student consultations, open forums, at online suggestion platforms para marinig ang tunay na saloobin ng mga estudyante. Kasama rin dito ang paglabas ng general plan of actions, budget breakdowns, at accomplishment reports upang mas mapanatili ang tiwala ng bawat estudyante sa konseho.
“Pangatlo, mas makabuluhang events para sa lahat. Gagawin nating inclusive at meaningful ang bawat SSC-led event. Hindi lang ito basta kasiyahan dahil gagawa rin po tayo ng mga aktibidad na magtataguyod ng leadership, advocacy, at student development.”
“Pang apat, pagpapalakas ng bawat student organization sa bawat departamento. Bilang inyong chairperson, sisiguraduhin kong makakaramdam ng buong suporta ang bawat departamental organization,” dagdag pa niya.
Paalala naman ng bagong SSC Chairperson na ang kaniyang pagkapanalo noong nakaraang eleksyon ay hindi lamang tagumpay ng isang tao kundi tagumpay rin ng bawat estudyante na naniniwala na pwedeng marinig, makilahok, at maging bahagi ng pagbabago.
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

# 10 HEIs sa Central Luzon, pasok sa Times Higher Education Impact Rankings 2025
[Image: 10 HEIs sa Central Luzon, pasok sa Times Higher Education Impact Rankings 2025]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, June 23, 2025**
Muling pinatunayan ng Central Luzon ang galing nito sa larangan ng edukasyon matapos mapasama ang 10 pampublikong Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) sa Times Higher Education (THE) Impact Ranking 2025.
Batay sa social media post ng Commission on Higher Education (CHED) Regional Office III, kabilang sa prestihiyosong listahan ang mga sumusunod na institusyon:
1. Central Luzon State University (801-1000)
2. Tarlac Agricultural University (1001-1500)
3. Bataan Peninsula State University (1501+)
4. Bulacan State University (1501+)
5. Don Honorio Ventura State University (1501+)
6. Nueva Ecija State University of Science and Technology (1501+)
7. Tarlac State University (1501+)
8. Gordon College (1501+)
9. Mabalacat City College (1501+)
10. City College of San Fernando, Pampanga (1501+)
Ang THE Impact Rankings ay sumusukat sa kontribusyon ng mga kolehiyo at unibersidad sa pag-abot ng United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), gaya ng dekalidad na edukasyon, gender equality, climate action, at iba pa.
Layunin nitong kilalanin at ipagdiwang ang mga institusyong aktibong nakikibahagi sa pagsusulong ng mas maayos at mas inklusibong mundo.
Ngayong taon, higit 2,000 universities mula sa 125 na bansa at teritoryo ang lumahok sa ranking. Sa bilang na ito, nasa 121 na institusyon sa Pilipinas ang kinilala, patunay ng lumlawak na global recognition pagdating sa kalidad ng edukasyon sa bansa.
Courtesy of CLTV36, click [here](https://cltv36.tv/10-heis-sa-central-luzon-pasok-sa-times-higher-education-impact-rankings-2025/?fbclid=IwY2xjawLhlMJleHRuA2FlbQIxMQABHjuiWKrpyze2X0ey_w9YqcxzFYqj-vVsA6VgMEYf5A0ksmU46n6TdywcL5ki_aem_Cu5pDIot_WbL-AJyV822ug) to read the full article.

# HON. ROLEN C. PAULINO JR. RECEIVES THE MODEL LOCAL CHIEF EXECUTIVE AWARD FOR 2025 FROM ALCUCOA
[Image: HON. ROLEN C. PAULINO JR. RECEIVES THE MODEL LOCAL CHIEF EXECUTIVE AWARD FOR 2025 FROM ALCUCOA]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, December 9, 2025**
Huge congratulations to our visionary City Mayor, Hon. Rolen C. Paulino Jr., who was conferred the prestigious Model Local Chief Executive Award for 2025 by the Association of Local Colleges and Universities Commission on Accreditation (ALCUCOA) on December 9, 2025, at the Diamond Hotel in Manila.
Our City Mayor received this top honor in recognition of his exemplary leadership and outstanding commitment to advancing quality and accessible higher education in Olongapo. His administration was specifically lauded for successfully elevating academic standards and modernizing campus infrastructure to secure a globally competitive educational landscape for the city’s constituents.

# THE FOREFRONT WINS BIG AT 5TH AOCPA
[Image: THE FOREFRONT WINS BIG AT 5TH AOCPA]
[ForeFront](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/forefront/) [OSWS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/osws/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, February 13, 2025**
SAN FERNANDO, Pampanga – The Forefront, the official student publication of Gordon College, garnered five awards at the 5th Assumption Outstanding Campus Press Awards (AOCPA) held at the University of the Assumption on January 27, 2025.
Thirty publications across Luzon participated in the competition, submitting a total of 278 entries according to Regina, the official student publication of the University of the Assumption.
The Forefront’s award-winning staff members are:
Patrisha Marie Pineda – 4th Most Outstanding Investigative Journalist
John Carlo Bermudez – 8th Most Outstanding Literary Writer
Stephanie Estrella – 8th Most Outstanding Opinion Writer
Lance Isaac Leon – 9th Most Outstanding Editorial Cartoonist
John Patrick Mateo – 10th Most Outstanding Feature Writer
The conference is an annual iteration of UA’s Regina in search for the most outstanding campus journalists in different categories across Luzon.

# GCAT result, available within 3 days — ATU
[Image: GCAT result, available within 3 days — ATU]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Saturday, February 21, 2026**
OLONGAPO CITY, Philippines—Admission and Testing Unit (ATU) Coordinator Christine Moselina said on Monday, February 16, that the Gordon College Admission Test (GCAT) result will be available within three-working days after taking the exam.  
In an improvement over the previous week-long release period, the office has shortened the turnaround time of the admission test’s results to three days after examination.  
“Maximum na submission ng result [is] 3 days, ma-u-upload na sa [Gordon College Admission (GCA)] portal nila ‘yong kanilang result. Ang waiting game diyan is ‘yong evaluation [from] coordinator,” Moselina said.  
She also noted that the new system reverses the usual flow: applicants must provide their details and requirements, including their Grade 11 records instead of Grade 12 for initial review before they can sign up for a new GCA account.  
“Before, ang dami kasing nagkakaroon ng problems with regard to the submission ng requirements. [May mga] nakapag-register pero ‘yong requirements kulang-kulang, so kami ang nahihirapan sa admissions.”  
However, applicants are still required to provide their Grade 12 records to their coordinators for evaluation at the time of their interview.  
As of February 20, with a week remaining until the expected end, the office has logged 3,930 registrations, compared to the 5,666 total applicants recorded last year.  
“GCAT natin is until June 30 pa, matagal-tagal pa. Madadagdagan pa ‘yan,” the coordinator said.  
The admission unit opened early online applications for a three-month window, running from November 27 through February 27. | via Keane Rafael Abdon, THE FOREFRONT
