### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/website-policy

# Website Policy
### Website Committee
Gordon College (GC) Website Committee was formally established by Interoffice Memorandum No. 38 Re: Website Committee. The Interoffice Memorandum states the following; composition, duties, and responsibilities of Website Committee, Guidelines in Website Content Development and Management, and Procedure for Posting/Uploading Web Content.
### Composition
GC Website Committee will be composed of a Chairperson, Vice-Chairperson, Website Administrator, and Members.  
For the members, it will be the Head of the Information and Communication Services Unit (ICSU), one (1) faculty member from each department designated by the College Dean, and one (1) staff from each office/unit assigned by the Unit Head.
### Duties and Responsibilities of Website Committee
Based on Interoffice Memo No. 38, the following are the duties and responsibilities of the committee:
1. Develop and recommend web related policies and standards
2. Oversee the design and underlying technology
3. Ensure the website stays current with new technology/trends
4. Implement regular data collection methods
5. Use data collected to guide the framework and infrastructure of the college website
6. Create guiding principles to steer the framework and infrastructure decisions of the college website
7. Work with the Management Information System Unit (MISU) to set timelines for web engine upgrade and new website deployments.
8. Regularly assess the design of the website and make recommendations for improvement
9. Serve as the liaison between the College President and the different department regards content
10. Identify and advocate for web needs and services for the college community.
### Guidelines in Website Content Development and Management
1. Interoffice Memo No. 38, clearly states the guidelines to be observed by the committee in terms of the contents for the website.
2. All news, updates and all other content to be posted on the official website should have clearance from concerned departments/ units and approval from the College President.
3. The Website Committee is tasked to manage the postings on the official website in coordination with the Information and Communication Services Unit (ICSU). The committee shall formulate posting guidelines and ensure proper dissemination.
4. Queries, concerns or issues aired through the official website must be communicated to concerned departments through ICSU who shall also responsible for posting official responses to such queries, concerns or issues whether through online or offline as the situation warrants.
### Procedure for Posting/Uploading Web Content
The following are the guidelines stated in Interoffice Memo No. 38 in terms of posting/uploading content to the college website:
1. Requesting department should submit a copy of the post to ICSU for approval of the College President.
2. After approval, ICSU shall transmit the content to the Website Committee Chairperson and Web Administrator.
### Website Content Submission
The GC Website Committee is pleased to offer the opportunity to share knowledge and expertise by submitting content to the college website with the following guidelines:
* **Guiding Principles**  
  Gordon College is one of the institutions that promote excellent education in Region III and the country. As such, the committee would like to publish and post content, materials, and ideas that support the vision, mission, and goals of the college. The committee also encourages each individual from different colleges and departments to share their experience and insights as one of the people of Gordon College.
* **Style**  
  The Website committee encourages each and everyone to use the current best practices for web writing. These includes:
  + Begin with the conclusion by focusing on the main message and be clear on how you want the reader to feel and respond.
  + Add specific details, facts, figures, quotes, etc.
  + Submit at least one (1) graphs, graphics, or photos that are copyright free with a size of;
    - 1280 pixels by 720 pixels for Hero Banners (Congratulatory Posters)
    - 1200 pixels by 630 pixels for News, Announcement and Notice
    - 250 pixels by 250 pixels for Website Banner
    - 250 pixels by 100 pixels for Logo (Rectangle)
    - 100 pixels by 100 pixels for Logo (Square)
  + Graphs, graphics or photos should be in .jpeg, .png or .svg format
  + Use subheadings that reflect the content and captures the interests of readers
  + Use listed points with ten (10) words or less per item
  + Search the web for other pro tips on best practices
* **Publishing**  
  The committee does not guarantee that it will post your submissions automatically. All published contents shall be well-guided and must receive the approval of concerned departments, as stated on Interoffice Memorandum No. 38.It does guarantee, however, that the article will be read and explore whether or not it is a good fit for the college and the readership. In reviewing submissions, the committee is looking for content that is necessary and relevant to the needs of the stakeholders, especially with the faculty and students. Articles reflecting the inspiring, powerful, responsible, productive, and connected nature of the academe are the ones that we are looking for.
* **Copyright**  
  The committee will only post copyright-free materials submitted by the author and will not pay for articles posted on the website. The college reserves the right to use the submitted article or any part of it depending on the committee’s discretion.
* **Response Time**  
  The goal of the committee is to respond to the requestor as quickly as possible. Depending on the situation, the committee will do its best to respond to each submission in 1 to 3 days.
* **Requestor**  
  The following groups and/or individuals are allowed to submit content for posting:
  + Department Heads
  + Vice Presidents
  + Faculty Members and
  + Administrative Staff
  + Recognized Student Organizations
  + Alumni
### Specific Guidelines
To achieve uniformity and establish the visual identity of the college kindly follow the following in submitting your content;
* **Title and Author Information**  
  All submission must identify (to be written in the body of your message);
  + Topic
  + Title
  + Author’s Full name
  + Author’s Phone Number\*
  + Author’s Email Address\*
  + Department (if applicable)  
    *\* – needed for communication purposes*
* **Summary**  
  Provide a 20-30 words summary of the article that can be used for links.
* **Length of the Content**  
  Submit articles that are between 500-750 words. Please submit final drafts that have proofread, and grammar and spelling checked.  
  Do not include the graphics or photos within the document. It should be saved separately in the same folder where the file resides.
* **Formatting**  
  Send your document at **biron.neilmarc@gordoncollege.edu.ph** with the following format details:
  + Subject : Request for Posting (Website)
  + Body : Author’s Information
  + Topic : Event/ Celebration
  + Title : Sportsfest
  + Author’s Full name : Juan Dela Cruz
  + Author’s Phone Number : 607.7175
  + Author’s Email Address : juandelacruz@gmail.com
  + Department : College of Allied Health Studies
  + Summary of your article.
  + Attachment:
    - document file of your article
    - picture1.jpg\* / picture1.png\* / picture1.svg\*  
      *\* – you can select one particular image type*
* **Links**  
  Provide a list of referenced links in the text or at the end of the submitted article. The committee will review these links to determine if they are appropriate for the website and will determine if they will be included or not (especially for copyright acknowledgment).
* **Categorization of Submission**  
  Articles that will be posted in the website can be categorized into;
  + News
  + Announcement or Guidelines
  + Notice or Advisory
* **Tags**  
  This will be use to sub-categorized your submission;
  + CAHS *(College of Allied Health Studies)*
  + CCS *(College of Computer Studies)*
  + CBA *(College of Business and Accountancy)*
  + CEAS *(College of Education, Arts, and Sciences)*
  + CHTM *(College of Hospitality and Tourism Management)*
  + IGS (*Institute of Graduate Studies)*
  + OSWS*(Office of Student Welfare and Services)*
  + Campus
  + Alumni
  + Administration
  + Academics
  + Forefront
### Content Administration
All web content submitted must be approved prior to posting. The following individuals retain the right to edit or request changes:
* College President
* Website Administrator
* Head of ICSU
* Vice Presidents
* Requestor/Author of the Content
### Website Content Update/ Revision
Thou postings are checked for spelling, grammar and factual accuracy, there will be some instances that can result unwanted errors.
The GC Website Committee will allow update or revision of articles posted on the website for the following reasons;
* Missing or Duplicate Titles
* Misspelled names
* Overlooked misspelled words or grammatical errors
* Missing images or links
* Missing details or information
### Website Content Removal
The GC Website Committee reserves the right to remove any posted content that contains copyrighted materials which include, but not limited to: text, images, graphics, sound recordings, video recordings or software programs.

# Gordon College Celebrates 26th Founding Anniversary with Successful FUNdation Run 2025
[Image: Gordon College Celebrates 26th Founding Anniversary with Successful FUNdation Run 2025]
[Alumni](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/alumni/) [Campus](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/campus/) [GCFAS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/gcfas/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, February 28, 2025**
Gordon College launched its 26th Founding Anniversary festivities through FUNdation Run 2025, which took place on February 23rd.
Gordon College Faculty Association (GCFAS), in partnership with Gordon College Alumni Association Inc. (GCAAI), successfully organized the event as many students, faculty members, alumni, and community members attended the celebration.
Among the distinguished guests present were Hon. Sarah Lugerna Lipumano-Garcia, Olongapo City Councilor; Dr. Imelda D.P. Soriano, College President; Ms. Agnes L. Melo, GCFAS President; and Mr. Joseph Angelo B. Pusing, GCAAI President.
They joined GCFAS and GCAAI officers, college officials, administrative staff, faculty, students, alumni, and numerous college supporters in making the event a resounding success.
Jeian Espiritu emerged as the winner in the Boys Student Category, completing the race in an impressive 10 minutes and 44 seconds.
“I was motivated to run by my teammates in the Gordon College athletics program and my girlfriend,” Espiritu shared. “Winning feels great but also adds pressure for future years.”
In the women’s Student Category, Susie May Mistica Ariño took first place with a time of 14 minutes and 43 seconds.
Ariño credited her partner’s encouragement and her focus on mental health for her success. “It started when my partner encouraged me to run,” Ariño explained.
“While running, I realized I wanted more than just a casual run for the sake of my mental health. I started joining his training plans until he gifted me a running watch.”
Furthermore, Nicole Bernal Marcial, an alumna of Gordon College, won the Alumni Category with a time of 14 minutes and 32 seconds.
“The FUNdation Run isn’t just about promoting fitness and health—it’s a way to celebrate and commemorate Gordon College’s 26th founding anniversary,” Marcial said.
“As an alumnus, I wanted to be part of this special occasion.”
He also emphasized the importance of community and the joy of running for his participation.
The winners also offered advice to aspiring runners.
Espiritu encouraged them to persevere even when facing challenges.
Meanwhile, Ariño stressed the importance of preparation and a winner’swinner’s mindset.
Lastly, Marcial advised runners to enjoy the process and find their motivation. “The hardest part of joining fun runs or any running event is finding the motivation to start. But once you understand why you’re doing it and learn to love the experience, everything else follows, ” he said.
He added, “One foot in front of the other, and always be proud of yourself for taking that step.”
The FUNdation Run 2025 served as a fitting start to Gordon College’s 26th Founding Anniversary, as it brought together the college community and promoted health and wellness.

# GCAAI accepts partnership with Lighthouse Bible Baptist Church and The Great Lighthouse Foundation, Inc
[Image: GCAAI accepts partnership with Lighthouse Bible Baptist Church and The Great Lighthouse Foundation, Inc]
[Alumni](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/alumni/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, December 18, 2023**
In a heartfelt recognition ceremony on December 16, 2023, the Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc. (GCAAI) was honored as one of the Ministry Partner by Lighthouse Bible Baptist Church and The Great Lighthouse Foundation, Inc. This prestigious acknowledgment and appreciation is a testament to the enduring collaboration between the two entities, fostering a spirit of community service and shared impact.
Over the year, GCAAI and Lighthouse Bible Baptist Church have embarked on several community outreach initiatives that go beyond the traditional boundaries of alumni engagement. These collaborative efforts include vital programs such as Basic Life Support Training, Brigada Eskwela Medical Mission, and the Community Wellness and Outreach Program, among others. These endeavors underline the commitment of both organizations to making a positive difference in the lives of the community they serve.
Mr. Joseph Pusing, Vice President of GCAAI, proudly accepted the award on behalf of the association, accompanied by fellow working committee members, Mr. Kenneth Bautista and Ms. Denise Punzalan.
As we express our gratitude for this significant recognition, GCAAI looks forward to further strengthening its partnership with Lighthouse Bible Baptist Church and The Great Lighthouse Foundation, Inc. This accolade not only celebrates past achievements but also serves as a motivation to continue impactful community service initiatives in the future.
GCAAI extends heartfelt thanks to Lighthouse Bible Baptist Church for their continuous support, and we are excited about the prospect of making an even more profound impact on the community through shared vision and collaboration.

# THE 4TH GORDON COLLEGE DISTINGUISHED ALUMNI SERVICE AWARD: Call for Nomination 2025
[Image: THE 4TH GORDON COLLEGE DISTINGUISHED ALUMNI SERVICE AWARD: Call for Nomination 2025]
[Alumni](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/alumni/)
**Monday, August 4, 2025**
**GENERAL INFORMATION AND GUIDELINES**
Pursuant to Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc. (GCAAI) Board Resolution No. 4, series of 2022, the GCAAI establishes the DISTINGUISHED ALUMNI SERVICE AWARD (DASA) to honor Gordon College graduates who have excelled both in their professional and personal careers and who have served as an example to other alumni, students, and the community, both locally and globally. Starting this year, the GCAAI Board of Trustees will recognize the exemplary service and distinguished achievements that brought honor to the Institution and have made significant contributions to society. The GC-DASA recipients will exemplify the core values that the Institution uphold since its establishment: Exceptional Character, Remarkable Excellence, And Unselfish Service.  
The GC-DASA shall be the highest honor bestowed by the Alumni Association and will be an event to be celebrated annually as part of the GC Founding Anniversary and/or GC Alumni Homecoming.  
**GENERAL GUIDELINES:**
1. Only graduates of Gordon College (or GC, formerly Olongapo City Colleges) are eligible for the GC-DASA. The nominee shall be a member of the Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc. as confirmed by its Board.
2. Any GC graduate, faculty member, or stakeholders may make nominations for the GC-DASA. Self–  
   nominations are accepted. The nominee shall accomplish the Official Nomination Form to be provided by the GCAAI. No limitation will be placed on the total number of nominations accepted for consideration.
3. The nominee shall have no pending administrative or criminal case upon nomination. He/she shall exhibit a high degree of character & integrity.
4. Only one recipient per college/program will be awarded. In case the college/program will have no  
   nominees/recipients, the awarding ceremony will still commence. Likewise, the GCAAI Board of Trustees can directly, without prejudice to the respective college, nominate and evaluate for such an award though the college/program have already a candidate.
5. Only living candidates are considered. In the event of the death of a candidate between notification of the award and the award ceremony, the award may be given posthumously with the permission of the family and the GCAAI. In the event that the awardee declines to accept the award and/or fails to attend the awarding ceremonies, the GCAAI reserves the right to withdraw the award.
6. A GC graduate sitting as a member of the selection committee or a member of the GCAAI Board of Trustees is not eligible to receive the award. This individual is eligible for nomination after a one-year waiting period from the date he/she steps down from his/her role.
7. Recipients are only eligible for one GC-DASA in a lifetime.
8. The recipients will receive a plaque or certificate of recognition and will be posted on the official FB page of the GCAAI. It will announce the annual recipients through various methods and honor each recipient with the appropriate recognition.
9. If for any reason, the GCAAI is not able to conduct the Awards as planned (including due to tampering, unauthorized intervention, fraud, technical difficulties, pandemic, or any other causes beyond the control of the organizer which impact the administration, security, fairness, integrity or proper conduct of the Awards), then they reserve the right in their sole discretion to make any reasonable variation to the Awarding Ceremony.
10. The Nomination Committee and/or GCAAI Board of Trustees may disqualify any nominee that they  
    reasonably determine has breached any of the terms and conditions or otherwise engaged in unlawful or other improper conduct that may bring the Awards into disrepute or jeopardize the fair and proper conduct of the Awards.
11. Other guidelines may be promulgated as decided by the GCAAI Board of Trustees.
**NOMINATION COMMITTEE:**  
The Nomination Committee (NC) may be organized by the Dean/OIC in every college and/or the GCAAI. The committee must be composed of at least three (3) members from their respective colleges. The total count of committee members should be an odd number. The chairperson of the NC shall be the Dean or its duly authorized representative and for the GCAAI, any member can initiate such nomination. No member of the NC must be related within the second degree of consanguinity or affinity to any of the nominees/candidates for awards. If the Dean/OIC is related within the second degree of consanguinity or affinity to any of the candidates for awards, the Dean/OIC must inhibit him/herself from participating in the process. The approval shall come from the person next in rank.  
The NC shall:
1. Abide by the criteria set forth by the GCAAI.
2. For nominees endorsed by the College, it shall issue a certificate of authenticity to all the documents that will be submitted to the GCAAI.
3. Deliberate on the qualifications of the nominees for the awards based on the criteria and the documents submitted.
4. Recommend to the GCAAI Board of Trustees or its Alumni Association President the result of nomination for approval.
5. Recommend to the GCAAI Board of Trustees or its Alumni Association President the resolution on any related issue that may arise from the results of the awards.
6. Ensure that the guidelines stipulated herein are followed.
**NOMINATION AND SELECTION PROCESS:**
1. Complete the online/hard copy Nomination Form.
2. Nominators will receive an auto-generated copy via email of what they submitted, i.e., the nomination form including any attachments. In any case, it is not available, the NC may determine what means of notification it will conduct to the nominators to confirm the submission of the nomination form and its attachments.
3. Nominees and nominators will be contacted by the NC/GCAAI within three days of receipt of the  
   nomination to acknowledge receipt.
4. A nominee may be asked to clarify or submit further information and evidence to assist the NC/GCAAI in assessing the nomination at any stage of the selection process.
5. The NC/GCAAI may also seek verification of the nominee’s achievements from their current or previous employer or other relevant individual or organization representative. By being nominated for this Award, the nominees are giving consent to the NC/GCAAI to contact these individuals to verify nominations.
6. Nominations will be reviewed and shortlisted by the NC/GCAAI and nominees who have not been  
   shortlisted will be notified. The GC-DASA candidates will be asked to submit a brief profile for this purpose. The candidate shall garner a total score of 60 points to be included in the shortlist. If a nominee cannot meet the required points, he/she is automatically excluded from the shortlist.
7. Every member of the NC will recommend/endorse the final candidate to the GCAAI President and then, the GCAAI Board of Trustees, sitting as Awards Selection Committee, will issue a confirmation through a Board Resolution. The report on the results of the NC shall be signed by all members of the committee and certified by the chairperson.
8. In any case that such protest will be filed during the confirmation of the final candidates, the final decision on the protest shall be decided by the GCAAI Board of Trustees. The GCAAI Board of Trustees’ decision is final on all matters relating to the nomination process and the selection of award recipients, and they are not required to provide feedback as to why a nomination was unsuccessful.
Call for Nominations: 4th Distinguished Alumni Service Award (DASA)
The Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc. proudly announces the opening of nominations for the 4th Distinguished Alumni Service Award (DASA), which will run from August 4 to 22, 2025. This prestigious recognition honors outstanding alumni who have demonstrated exceptional service, leadership, and commitment to their profession, community, or alma mater. We encourage all alumni, faculty, and stakeholders to submit nominations for individuals who embody the values and excellence upheld by Gordon College. For complete nomination guidelines, kindly scan the QR code below.
The awarding ceremony will be held on October 11, 2025. Let us come together to recognize and celebrate the achievements of Gordon College alumni who continue to make a lasting difference.

### Source: https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/campus-life/library-and-instructional-media-center/limc-history

# LIMC History
Gordon College Library started in 1999 when the former Olongapo City Colleges, now Gordon College, put up a curricular program for Computer Studies and Business Management and Accountancy. With only a few books and mostly computer subjects in hand, Mrs. Remedios Sapuan, who was then a Utility worker, took charge of the library operation with the assistance of the Administrative Staff.
The Library moved four times to different locations in the building and its fourth was in the former KHG Hall. When the library was first opened, books were in the cabinet with glass doors and were locked. The library was using the close shelf system, where stacks cannot be directly accessed by users. The students should approach the assigned staff when borrowing books and it was then the bookshelf was opened to get the book needed.
In 2002, Thelma Ramos, a clerk aid of the Assessor’s Office, was transferred to the College Library to assist Mrs. Sapuan in the library operation. According to Mrs. Sapuan, a certain instructor, in the person of Mr. Sydney Maglalang, was temporarily assigned to the library. Services of the staff in the City Library were borrowed in the processing of books acquired for the College Library. In June 2003, the first College Librarian was hired, Ms. Maria Corazon D. Maneja as a casual position. Later she was promoted to a permanent position. The problem was by her was the book organization, the right classification of books, classification tools (DDC) Dewey Decimal Classification System and there were no catalog cards and cabinets. Some of the books were infested by termites which were already problems before but were not treated completely. Books were thenr eorganized, classified, and cataloged and were assigned new numbers using DD Classification tools that were borrowed from the Ci4ty Library. The circulation counter and grills dividing the books stacks and reading area were removed. Shelves were distributed and fastened on all library walls to make the books accessible to the users and to widen the reading area. Heavily termite-infested books were removed and deleted from the records. New books were formed. Newspapers were compiled with the date labeled and put on display. In 2006, books from Australia were received by the library through the Rotary Club of Olongapo. Books were found to be outdated and mostly for High School and Elementary level students. In the same year, another book donation was received from Asia Foundations, Inc. Books became very useful to patrons because of their general content. In March 2007, Ms. Betty Fielder, a friend of Mrs. Dely Aristorenas, former College Administrator, donated books and magazines for use by HRM students and faculty. Two air conditioners were installed to replace the former electric fans for ventilation and besides its small space, library patrons flocked the library especially nursing students who occupied the most number of enrollees. The library had then 8,361 volumes of books as of October 2007.
In the summer of 2010, the librarian during one of the EXECOM (ExecutiveCommittee) meetings, suggested removing over duplicated copies of books to provide spaces for new arrivals. Enough copies from weeded books were retained. 2,958 books were discarded and returned to GSO (Government Supply Office) but the latter suggested just donating them to the City Library.
In 2012, Gordon College&#39;s 13th Founding Anniversary, a Give-a-Book Project was launched and spearheaded by the College Library to collect tertiary level books from students, faculty, alumni, and friends of Gordon College. As of this date, May 6, 2013, we have collected 182 book donations.
On August 7, 2012, when the first knee-deep flood entered the College Library, books located on the first two-row level shelves were partially damaged but were restored. But on September 23, 2013, during a chest-deep heavy flood of Olongapo City, almost 2/3 of the library materials were soaked in the mud and water and devastated and discarded. Furniture which was made of light materials, files, and newly acquired books was included, but other computers were restored. Due to the kindness of City Mayor Rolen C. Paulino, he approved a 1 million budget for the purchase of new general education books, professional books, and journals. The first set of books was delivered in December 2013. Another era was opened for Gordon College Library for its transfer to the new building funded and built by SM in 2015-2016. The official transfer began on June 24, 2016, until July of the same year. The new and much wider library which is located on the fourth floor was opened to students on August 1, 2016, the start of the first semester, the Academic Year 2016- 2017.
Mr. Marlon A. Atencio recommended to the Executive Council the change of the library name into “Library and Instructional Media Center” for the reason that the library is now having a media center that supports the educational needs of the library and it was approved by the Executive Council spearheaded by the College President, Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano last May 31, 2018.

i

About the Journal
SIHAY is the official research journal of science education of Gordon
College. The name of the journal originated from the Filipino word “sihay”
which translate to English word as “cell”, symbolizing the basic functional
unit of life paying homage to Robert Hooke’s observation of box-shape
structure of cork cell which he called it “cells” as it reminds him of the
rooms, or cells. We should recognize the classroom as the nucleus for
fostering scientific curiosity and knowledge. Embracing this metaphorical
richness, SIHAY is dedicated to exploring the complexities of science
education within the classroom settings.
Within its pages, SIHAY explores into innovative teaching strategies,
integration of technology in teaching, curriculum development strategies,
and the likes to enhance the science learning experience. With a
commitment to equity and excellence, SIHAY endeavors to advance the
field of science education, empowering educators and learners alike to
navigate the ever-evolving landscape of scientific inquiry and discovery.
ii

iii

The Utilization of Strategic Intervention Materials in Enhancing the
Academic Performance of Eighth Grade Students in Chemistry
Lawrence Bianes 1
Cristina S. Noble2
Jason M. Yap 3
1Student Teacher: BSEd-Science, Gordon College
2Teacher-III, Olongapo City National High School
3Program Coordinator: BSEd-Science, Gordon College
ABSTRACT
Chemistry is a branch of science that is often associated with learning gaps that
affects the academic performance in certain learning areas. These gaps, if left
untreated, negatively affect students, and cause them to not attain fundamental
skills and knowledge which are necessary to higher grade levels. This study
aims to enhance the academic performance of the students by addressing the
issues in learning gaps observed by the students of Olongapo City National High
School. The thirty students, which are chosen through purposive sampling, first
went through course discussion. Afterward, they were subjected to pre-
assessment wherein they did not meet expectations with their average score of
58.03 (56.34%). The results were taken for item analysis that identified the
learning areas with adversities and became the focus of the contents of “Finding
Elementary: A Strategic Intervention Material in Science VIII.” The material
was then implemented for a week then followed by post-assessment where an
improvement was recorded with their fairly satisfactory performance with an
average score of 80.63 (78.28%). After the normal results with the Shapiro-Wilk
Test of Normality, the Wilcoxon Signed Rank Test indicated a statistically
significant difference between the pre-assessment and post-assessment of the
students implicating the positive effect of the applied intervention. To further
develop the potential of the material formulated, the research suggests gathering
the perspective of the students on the material for additional constructive
criticisms to consider, for future purposes.
Keywords: Photovoice, Academic Performance, General Biology I
INTRODUCTION
The Philippines has a long-lasting national curriculum that further divides
schools into Kindergarten, Elementary, Secondary, and Senior High levels. In
these different Levels, the same core subjects are applied, which include English,
Filipino, Mathematics, Physical Education and Arts, and Science. These are
1

compulsory subjects that must be taken regardless of their grade level and the
program they are enrolled in.
One of these core subjects, science, in definition, is a system of knowledge
covering general truths or the operation of general laws especially as obtained and
tested through scientific method (Merriam Webster, 2023). Since science is a
complex and broad subject of study, in the country’s curriculum, its four basic
branches — Physics, Biology, Earth Science, and Chemistry, are allocated in each
of the four quarters of every academic year.
Science is divided into different branches that focus on different areas of
knowledge. Included in these branches is chemistry which deals with matter and
its interactions with other matter and energy. In eighth-grade curricula, where the
researcher was assigned to teach, chemistry was focused on the different phases of
Matter, the atoms and their sub-atomic particles, and the periodic table of elements.
Chemistry can be overwhelming to some and is often referred to as a
challenge (North Central College, 2021). In a different study conducted by Tilahun
et al. (2016), they concluded that the difficulties of chemistry topics are due to their
abstract nature and words from everyday language with different meanings. These
difficulties, if not overcome, will lead to failure in the examinations. In the same
study, they found out that usually, these gaps will continue without being corrected
because the teachers did not take any remedial actions to fill the gaps of slow
learner students. Furthermore, this lack of remedial actions can be rooted in the
lack of textbooks and other materials which is a common complaint among public
schools.
In the setting where the researcher of this study is deployed, the same
problem regarding unaddressed learning gaps in Chemistry was highly notable
through the students’ low scores in assessments indicating their low mastery of the
concepts. Through observations, it was noted how some learners failed to fully
comprehend basic chemistry topics and affected them to struggle in
comprehending the more complex concepts that build upon the fundamental
notions because they do not fully and adequately understand the fundamental
principles (Woldeamanuel et al., 2016).
To cite a result from the prior observations, the ninth-grade learners have
shown learning adversities in understanding the concept of chemical bonding,
since they have failed to master the concept of sub-atomic particles. In a crisis like
this, it is important to identify, with a sense of urgency, what topics the students
are having difficulty with, since the mastery of a certain lesson, is a pre-requisite
of another lesson in a higher level of education.In addressing issues with learning
gaps, Doroliat et al. (2018) emphasized in their study that every class should
include addressing the misunderstandings of the students. A learner won't be able
to completely understand and explain other concepts if they continue to hold false
2

beliefs about the previous lesson. As per the Department of Education's mission,
teachers facilitate learning and constantly nurture every learner.
Aimed to avoid future problems in Chemistry at a higher level of education
rooted in low mastery level at the previous level of education, this research was
designed to ensure that the students were equipped with the fundamental concepts
by identifying what lessons have faced learning adversities and enhancing them
through the determined and formulated intervention.
The intervention proposed by this research is the implementation of
Strategic Intervention Materials (SIM) to enhance the least learned competencies.
A pre-assessment was conducted which results were organized in a t-table, an
innovation by the researcher to organize the discussed lessons based on the
mastery/ achievement level of the students. The results from this T-table decided
the concepts that were included in the SIM that was latterly implemented. These
are instructional tools designed to impart knowledge and abilities. Students were
offered resources to aid in the mastery of a competency-based skill that they were
unable to acquire through regular classroom instructions.
The study used the Input-Process-Output (IPO) model, which served as
the study's conceptual foundation. The model describes how the study will be
conducted.
STATEMENT OF THE PROBLEM
This study employed and measured the implemented strategic intervention
material in unraveling these identified learning adversities.
Specifically, the study aims to answer the following queries:
1. What are the scores of the students before the implementation of the
intervention?
2. What are the scores of the students after the implementation of the
intervention?
3. Is there any significant difference between the pre-assessment and post-
assessment scores of the class?
4. What are the implications of the results of implementing the strategic
Intervention materials?
METHODOLOGY
Research Design
The study employed causal hypotheses which proposed how the strategic
intervention material might have affected the performance of the students in certain
learning areas. According to White et al. (2014), causal hypotheses are tested in
quasi-experimental research designs. An "intervention" is evaluated in quasi-
3

experimental research based on how successfully it accomplishes its goals as
determined by a pre-determined set of indicators. By definition, a quasi-
experimental design lacks random assignment; rather, the participants are chosen
by an administrator. The study directly fell in this definition wherein the study
employed pre- and post-assessment scores for the effectiveness of the applied
intervention. Since the study aimed to measure the effectiveness of the intervention
through measurement of the pre-assessment scores against the post-assessment
scores, the entire thirty students of the class were encouraged to participate.
Respondents
The study employed one group: the experimental group. Participation
from the group was vital to the study since it investigated whether there are
differences between the results of their pre-assessment and post-assessment scores.
Thus, the study established a comparative analysis.
The experimental group was determined through the student teacher's
observation. Therefore, non-probability sampling was utilized rather than
randomized.
The participants of this study are enrolled in Olongapo City National High
School. Since only one group was necessary for this, among five sections handled
by the cooperating teacher, the group was drawn through careful observation and
decision. The decision was that the thirty Grade 8 - Gemino Abad students served
as the experimental group.
Research Instrument
The study employed different materials that were prepared by the
researcher and were reviewed and validated by the cooperating teacher, for the
revisions.
The pre-assessment and post-assessment questionnaires followed the same
format of two: multiple choice and fill-in-the-blanks that aimed to measure the
student's learning in different learning areas, specifically, atomic models and their
proponents, and the sub-atomic particles of an atom. A copy of the assessments
was included in the appendices section of the study.
The results of the pre-assessment underwent organization through the
innovative T-table, which the idea was defended during the first held defense.
Statistical Treatment of Data
The results of the pre-assessment and post-assessment, including the item
analysis, were tabulated using Microsoft Excel for frequency distribution, mean,
and descriptive interpretations to be done effectively. The descriptive
4

interpretations utilized in the study were adopted from DepEd Memorandum No.
160, S. 2012, and DepEd Order No. 8, S. 2015.
Grading Scales Description
90 – 100 Outstanding
85 – 89 Very Satisfactory
80 – 84 Satisfactory
75 – 79 Fairly Satisfactory
Below 75 Did not meet expectations
Table 1. Descriptors for Scores in Pre-Assessment and Post-Assessment
MASTERY/ACHIEVEMENT LEVEL
MPS Descriptive Equivalent
96 – 100% Mastered
86 – 95% Closely Approximating Mastery
66 – 85% Moving Towards Mastery
35 – 65% Average
15 – 34% Low

0 – 4% Absolutely No Mastery

RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
1. What are the students’ scores before the implementation of the
intervention?
A week of discussing the course was conducted. Afterward, the students
were subjected to an assessment to measure their learning in the discussion before
the implementation of the Strategic Intervention Material. The assessment served
also as an indicator of which of the lessons the students had difficulties with.
Score Percentage Descriptor
Highest Scored 87 84.47 Satisfactory
Student
Lowest Scored 19 18.45 Did not meet
Student expectations
Overall/ Class 58.03 56.34 Did not meet
Average expectations
Table 3. The Scores of the Students Before the Implementation of the SIM
5

The table above is a summary of the individual scores of the students,
while the detailed data were included in the appendices section of the study. From
the class, at that point, the highest score is 87 (84.47%), which indicates a
satisfactory performance. In comparison to this, the attained lowest score is 19
(18.455), indicating a performance that did not meet expectations. The overall
score of the class is 58.03 (56.34%) which directly states that the performance of
the class did not meet expectations. This result proves the existence of students
finding the learning area difficult to comprehend— leading gradually to learning
gaps. In a different study conducted by Gafoor et al., (2013), they also proposed
that students are indeed facing difficulties in understanding Chemistry concepts
and the teachers must introduce it as an intellectual pursuit, as creative science,
since most students found the concepts abstract and unrelated to the world, they
live in.
While there are students who scored fairly, the number of students who
did not meet expectations is concerning, and if left unturned, will affect them
negatively in the higher grade level. In addition, the possibility of it being
compounding should also be considered. If the concern in learning gaps is not
addressed with urgency, according to Torres (2021), learners will likely fail further
as time passes by because of the skills not attained prior and will pose a threat to
their progress to a higher level of the education ladder.
2. What are the scores of the students after the implementation of the
intervention?
The scores of the students in the assessment conducted after the Strategic
Intervention Material was introduced to the class, along with the thorough item
analysis, were the basis of the formulation of the SIM. The intervention took a
week to be completed. Since the study aims to promote an effective intervention
to solve the concern in learning gaps, the scores of the students after the
implementation were recorded.
Score Percentage Descriptor
Highest Scored 96 93.20 Outstanding
Student
Lowest Scored 40 38.83 Did not meet
Student expectations
Overall/ Class 80.63 78.28 Fairly
Average Satisfactory
Table 4. The Scores of the Students After the Implementation of the SIM
The table above is a summary of the progress of the students after a week
of working with the Strategic Intervention Material, while the detailed individual
6

scores were included in the appendices section of the study. From the class, at that
point, the highest score is 96 (93.20%), which indicates an outstanding
performance. In comparison to this, the attained lowest score is 40 (38.83%),
indicating a performance that did not meet expectations. The overall score of the
class is 80.63 (78.28%) which directly states that the performance of the class was
fairly satisfactory. These results showed commendable improvements after the
SIM was utilized in the class. These results can be further proven as significant by
citing the work of Suarez et al., (2020), wherein after the introduction of SIM to
their school, the experimental group improved their performance level and further
strengthened their proposition of SIM as an effective teaching strategy that aims
to positively address the problems students were facing in developing fundamental
knowledge and skills in the least mastered topics in science.
While their scores individually undoubtedly showed improvements, it is
also notable how these are relatively low based on the descriptors and can still be
improved. This implied how the applied intervention, despite producing good
results, can still be subjected to revisions to nurture its potential, for the benefit of
the students. The study of Kapur (2019), a different study, stated the same truth of
teaching-learning materials should always be subjected to development as these
enable students to learn efficiently and attain the set goals and objectives for every
learning area.
3. Is there any significant difference between the pre-assessment and post-
assessment scores of the class?
To test the efficacy of the applied SIM in addressing the learning gaps
identified by prior procedures, the pre-assessment and post-assessment scores of
the students were subjected to comparison through Wilcoxon Signed Rank Test, a
non-parametric test.
Assessment n Median z Asymp. Conclusion
Sign.
Pre 30 60.50 -4.682 .000 Significant
Post 30 81.00
Table 5. Difference between the Pre-Assessment and Post-Assessment Scores of the
Students
The table presents the Wilcoxon Signed Rank Test which highlighted a
statistically significant difference between the pre-assessment and post-assessment
scores of the students. It is revealed that the post-assessment score (Mdn = 81.00,
n = 30) was significantly higher after the intervention compared to the pre-
assessment score (Mdn = 60.50, n = 30), z = -4.682, p =.000, and large effect size
7

(r =.60) at the 5% significance level. These results implied that the null hypothesis
must be rejected.
These results have proven that the study has formulated and proposed an
effective intervention. The proposition was also supported by different correlated
studies that highlighted the potential of the utilization of the Strategic Intervention
Material, as proposed by the Department of Education (Salviejo et al., 2014).
Specifically, the study by Cordova et al., (2019) stated that to solve the concerning
performance of the students in terms of mastering competency-based skills,
strategic intervention materials should be introduced.
4. What are the implications of the results of implementing the Strategic
Intervention Materials?
The study has presented a commendable enhancement in students’ scores,
based on the class’ average that changed from 56.34% to 78.28%. This increase in
the scores proves that the strategic intervention material formulated, produced, and
implemented is effective.
This research aims to address the problem of the learning gaps in certain
learning areas which are fundamental to the following grade levels. This concern
became apparent in the Philippines' education for the past years due to the forced
school closures which are caused by the global pandemic (Lazo et al., 2021).
Studies, such as this, where interventions for the above-mentioned problem are
introduced provide an idea of what strategies have the potential to be prospered to
lead the students having adversities with learning competencies, in the future
(Acedillo et al., 2022). Since the study attained positive yet can still be developed
results, the utilized Strategic Intervention Material can be disseminated not only
throughout the school, but also to the neighboring schools, to enhance the students’
academic performance.
Conclusion
1. The students, before the intervention was implemented, had an overall
performance that did not meet expectations. This implied how learning
gaps are existent in certain learning areas in the course and it affected their
academic performance.
2. Properties of Sub-atomic particles, Models of an Atom Based on History,
Determining the Number of Protons, Neutrons, and Electrons of Elements,
and Determining the Number of Protons, Neutrons, Electrons, and Net
Charges of Isotopes—are the topics where the students performed
8

unsatisfactorily with an MPS of below 74%. This result was set as a
foundation for what focus or content the Strategic Intervention Material
should have.
3. The students, after the implementation phase, did fairly satisfactorily. This
indicated a developmental enhancement in the academic performances of
the students.
4. The result of the Wilcoxon Signed Rank Test with their pre-assessment
and post-assessment of the students concluded that there is a significant
difference between the two. This indicated that the implemented Strategic
Intervention Material was effective.
Recommendations
1. Upon further data analysis, the researcher measured the effectiveness of
the Strategic Intervention Material on the female students and compared it
with the male students. These results are presented in the appendices
section. The results have shown how the SIM is more effective for female
than male students. Since SIM is a visual-verbal instructional material and
no male student belongs to this kind, this is an aspect to consider that
affected their data. The study recommends the addition of activities that
also cater to learning styles—intrapersonal, interpersonal, logical, musical
rhythmic, bodily-kinesthetic, existentialist, and naturalist.
2. The intervention produced improvement and undeniably and positively
addressed learning gaps after being implemented in remedial classes. The
study recommends systemic remedial classes and the application of SIM
in the cooperating school and others to address the apparent decline in the
Philippines' education.
3. The SIM was validated by the cooperating teacher before it was produced
and implemented for possible revisions. In addition, the researcher
suggests surveying the students about their perceptions of the SIM applied
and these results will develop the potential of the SIM formulated, for
future purposes.

PHOTOVOICE: AN INNOVATIVE TOOL TO ENHANCE THE
ACADEMIC PERFORMANCE OF GRADE 12 STUDENTS IN GENERAL
BIOLOGY-I
Rheena Janelle E. Udan 1
Lea Ann P. Alegre 2
Jason M. Yap 3
1Student Teacher: BSEd-Science, Gordon College
2Master Teacher I: Senior High School, Regional Science High School III
3Program Coordinator: BSEd-Science, Gordon College
ABSTRACT
As a part of the Learning Continuity Plan (LCP) of the Department of Education,
K-12 Curriculum was restructured into the Most Essential Learning
Competencies (MELCs) resulting to the removal of several learning
competencies which made it difficult for students to connect lessons to other. In
this sense, the researchers aimed to adapt a methodology with the objective to
enhance the academic performance of the students in General Biology-I, called
Photovoice. Thus, the researchers administered a one-shot pre-test and post-test
that covered three topics which were identified as the least learned competencies
namely, Cell Cycle, Cell Transport Mechanism, and Cellular Respiration, to
assess the performance of the students before and after the intervention was
employed. A one-sample T-test was used to analyze the data gathered, where in
results showed that there is a significant difference between the pre-test and
post-test scores. This showed that there was an improvement in the students’
academic performance. However, a Pearson product moment correlation
revealed that the perception and post-test scores of the students have no
significant relationship due to different factors that may have contributed to their
performance. Nevertheless, the increase in the mean scores during the pretest
and posttest could not be neglected. The study recommends lengthening the
implementation of PhotoVoice to promote learning through real-life
experiences. Moreover, it also recommends giving full identification to the
results of this study to design more innovative instructions and strategies to
further improve teaching-learning process.
Keywords: Photovoice, Academic Performance, General Biology I
INTRODUCTION
Department of Education (DepEd), in response to the challenges the
pandemic had on education, had developed the Learning Continuity Plan (LCP).
Under this LCP is the implementation of remote learning which allowed flexibility
13

in time and place of students’ learning, including, but not limited to, the use of
technology. Though Information and Communications Technology (ICT) had
already been integrated to classrooms prior to pandemic, the situation made both
the teachers and students heavily relied more on the use of technology, particularly
smartphones, laptop, tablets, and other gadgets. Statista (2022) reports that there
are currently 6.648 billion smartphone users worldwide and 79 million in the
Philippines as of 2020. The two-year implementation of remote learning had
greatly influenced the smartphone dependence of the students (Saadeh et al., 2021;
Serra et. al., 2021).
Biology, like other science subjects, is a practical-oriented discipline
which seeks to develop the scientific inquiry and problem-solving skills of
learners. However, as a part of the LCP, K-12 Curriculum was streamlined into the
Most Essential Learning Competencies (MELCs) which was identified as the
competencies that a learner needs and important for them to acquire to have a
successful life. Many learning competencies were removed making it hard for
some students to connect present lessons to other concepts which was supposedly
included in the curriculum. Because of this, teaching Biology became challenging
because of the lack in practical or hands-on activities for the past two (2) years. To
enable the students to fully grasp the lessons, teachers need to create learning
environments that will associate the biology concepts with their real lives. It was
observed by the researchers that during Biology classes, learners show more
interest, and they easily understand the concepts when it is connected on what they
see on their daily lives.
The above information caught the attention of the researchers and thought
to adapt a methodology with the objective to enhance the academic performance
of the students in Biology, called Photovoice. Photovoice was first introduced by
Wang and Burris in 1992. It was utilized as a tool in the classroom to facilitate
substantial reflections using digital photography (Bazemore-Bertrand, 2021).
Through photovoice, students will make reflections and analyze their perspectives
to come up with descriptions evident in photos and images they have taken
(Titular, 2019). Additionally, photovoice can be utilized to encourage students'
critical thinking and the development of other abilities such as expressing opinions
and views, igniting creativity, honing research skills, fostering relationships with
others, and attracting attention (Andina-Díaz, 2020; Ciolan & Manasia, 2017;
Liebenberg, 2018, Simanjuntak, 2020). According to several studies, photovoice
can offer creative ways to develop lessons, and when used properly in the
classroom, it can successfully engage students and improve learning (Mildred, et.
al 2021, Aboulkacem, et. al, 2022).
Studies show that presenting students with activities where they could
connect what was being taught inside the classroom to their personal lives made
14

the learning experience more meaningful and increased their academic
engagement. In the process of PhotoVoice, the participants, who were also
observed to be having a hard time expressing themselves, will use voice recording
instead of the traditional written description. The said intervention was guided with
rubrics for both picture-taking and verbal descriptions and was strictly based on
the identified least learned competencies, namely Cell Division, Cell Transport
Mechanism, and Cell Respiration. Thus, with the aim to enhance the academic
performance of Grade 12 students of Regional Science High School III in General
Biology, the researchers adapted this method in the teaching-learning process.
STATEMENT OF THE PROBLEM
This study aims to determine the PHOTOVOICE as An Innovative Tool
to Enhance the Academic Performance of Grade 12 Regional Science High School
– III Students in General Biology-I for the S.Y. 2022-2023. Specifically, it sought
to answer the following questions:
1. How may the respondents’ perception of PhotoVoice as a tool in
understanding and mastery of General Biology concepts be described?
2. How may the respondents’ performance in General Biology be
described before and after using the PhotoVoice?
3. Is there a significant difference between the students’ performance in
terms of Pre-test and Post-test before and after using Photovoice?
4. Is there a significant relationship between the perception and the
respondents’ performance after using the PhotoVoice?
METHODOLOGY
Research Design
The study aimed to determine whether the PhotoVoice is an effective
innovative tool in Enhancing Academic Performance of Grade 12 RSHS-III
students in General Biology-I. The researchers used Quantitative-Descriptive as
the method of research.
According to an article recently published by Crossman (2018),
Quantitative research is a structured way of collecting and analyzing data obtained
from different sources. It involves the use of computational, statistical, and
mathematical tools to derive results. It is conclusive in its purpose as it tries to
quantify the problem and understand how prevalent it is by looking for projectable
results to a larger population.
Descriptive research is used in a wide variety of research methods that
aims to describe a population, situation, or phenomenon accurately and
15

systematically (McCombes, 2022). It is concerned with the conditions, differences,
and relationships that exist, opinions that are held, processes that are going on,
effects that are evident, or trends that are developing (Best & Kahn, 2014).
Respondents
The participants for this study consist of the Grade 12 students of Regional
Science High School-III enrolled during the school year 2022-2023. Particularly,
thirty (30) students from the different sections who were distinguished to be
struggling in certain concepts in General Biology as shown in Table 1.
Based on the results of the First Quarter Exam and the classification of
grades, these students were identified who are in need of learning intervention and
need to be recovered. They were the respondents as they are the students whose
grades fell under the satisfactory level (80-84).
Table 1: Distribution of Respondents
Grade 12
Section Frequency Percentage
Gauss 6 20
Napier 7 23
Riemann 9 30
Leibniz 8 27
Total 30 100
Research Instrument
This study utilized two (2) instruments, the pre- and post-test and the four
(4) point scale questionnaire on photovoice. In the first instrument, the pre-and
post-test consisted of 30-item questions regarding Cell Division, Cell Transport
Mechanism, and Cellular Respiration. This was administered to the students before
and after employing PhotoVoice.
The second instrument was the Likert Scale questionnaire which include
statements that assessed the students’ perception towards the use of PhotoVoice as
a learning tool which was given to the respondents after the post-test. This consist
of three parameters: (1) Convenience, Interest, Ease, and Effectiveness, (2)
Measurement and Academic Achievement, and (3) Communication and
Interaction.
Statistical Treatment of Data
16

The data gathered were recorded, tabulated, and will be analyzed through
statistical treatment using SPSS. The researchers used the descriptive, inferential
statistics and one shot experimental of Pre and Post test.
Descriptive statistics are used to summarize data in an organized manner
by describing the relationship between variables in a sample or population (Kaur,
et. al., 2018). This was used to describe the academic performance of the students
based on the pre-test and post-test results and the students’ perception towards the
use of PhotoVoice as a learning tool. The researchers utilized the following
descriptive statistical tools:
1. Mean. This was used to determine the average Pretest and Posttest scores
and the final weight of each item on the students’ perception in using
PhotoVoice.
2. Weighted Mean and Descriptive Rating. These were used to describe the
perception of the students in using Photovice. Based on the total scores,
the students’ perception will be quantified as follows:
Table 2: Score, Range, Descriptive Rating, and Interpretation
Descriptive Score Interval Descriptive Interpretation
Rating
Strongly 4 3.26-4.00 Learners have strong agreement
Agree that PhotoVoice contributed to
their performance.
Agree 3 2.51-3.25 Learners have an agreement that
PhotoVoice contributed to their
performance.
Disagree 2 1.75-2.50 Learners have disagreement that
PhotoVoice contributed to their
performance.
Strongly 1 1.00-1.75 Learners have strong disagreement
Disagree that PhotoVoice contributed to
their performance.
3. Shapiro-Wilk Test. This was used to test the normality of the data
collected. This normality test is required to determine the appropriate
statistical tool to be used to analyze the data. If the Sig. value of the
Shapiro-Wilk Test is greater than 0.05, the data is normally distributed.
If it is below 0.05, the data significantly deviate from a normal
distribution.
Inferential statistics is used to derive estimates about a population and draw
conclusions on the data based on hypotheses testing methods (CFI, 2022). In this
17

study, this will be used to test the hypothesis of the study. Particularly, the
difference of the students’ Pretest and Posttest scores before and after using the
Photovoice and the relationship between the students’ performance after the use of
Photovoice and the perception of the students. The inferential statistical tool that
will be used in this study are as follows:
1. One-Sample T-test. This was used to determine if there is a significant
difference between the students’ performance in terms of Pretest and
Posttest scores. The required level of significance is 95%.
2. Pearson’s Product Moment Correlation. This was used to test the
significant relationship between the students’ perception of PhotoVoice
and their perception in using it as a learning tool. This statistical tool was
used to determine whether the increase in the post-test scores are related
to the usage of PhotoVoice.
Results and Discussion
Table 3 shows that students have strong agreement that PhotoVoice helped
them with their performance in terms of Convenience, Interest, Ease, and
Effectiveness with a mean of 3.317, Measurement and Academic Achievement
with 3.333, and Communication and Interaction with 3.260. The Over-all, the
average weighted mean of 3.303 implies that PhotoVoice improves their over-all
understanding of the concepts covered, facilitates and enhances the interaction and
communication among the students, and contributes positively to their learning
experiences. The findings of this study is in line with the study conducted by
Younis (2021) which revealed that students perceived PhotoVoice as a stimulating
element in their learning experiences as they had to re-evaluate their ways of
thinking and doing things.

tool in understanding and mastery of General Biology concepts

The respondents’ performance in General Biology I before and after using
the PhotoVoice are reflected in Table 4. With a minimum score of 11, and a
18

maximum score of 19, results revealed that the posttest scores are higher
(M=14.800, s=2.058) than pretest scores (M=13.833, s=2.937) with 8 and 20 as
the minimum and maximum scores, respectively. The result implies that there is
an improvement on the academic performance of the learners based on the mean
scores. This is in line with the study conducted by Anwar (2021) where results
indicated that there is also an increase in the students’ pre-test and post-test scores
indicating that PhotoVoice helped to improve their academic performance.

Table 5 presents the test of normality of students’ performances in terms
of pre-test and post-test scores using the Shapiro-Wilk Test. As shown in the table,
both pretest (W=0.976, p-value=0.705) and post-test (W=0.961, p-value= 0.332)
which are both greater than the significance level of 0.05 indicating that the data
is normally distributed. Since the data do not significantly differ from normal
distribution, the researchers decided to use parametric test to test the difference,
specifically, one-sample t-test.

Table 6 shows the results of the test of difference or one-sample T-test
used by the researchers to analyze if PhotoVoice has any effect towards the
performance of Grade 12 students. Results revealed that the posttest scores
(M=14.800, s=2.058) after the intervention are higher than pretest scores
(M=13.833, s=2.937) before the intervention. Using these data, it is observed that
the post-test had significant difference for it is higher than the pre-test. In addition,
the computed p-value that is 0.000 is lower than the level of significance that is
19

0.05; hence, these data reveal that there is a significant difference between the
conducted pre-test and post-test, rejecting the null hypothesis. These findings
corroborate the results obtained by Anwar (2021) where there is a significant
difference after having treatment by PhotoVoice indicating that students who were
taught by using PhotoVoice achieved better score.

Test
Table 7 presents the Pearson Product Moment Correlation between the
respondents’ perceptions and performance after using the PhotoVoice. As shown,
there is no significant relationship between the posttest scores and perception based
on the p-value of 0.341, thus, null hypothesis is not rejected. This result implies
that there may be different factors that contributed to the increase of the mean
scores of the students other than PhotoVoice such as the students’ personality,
academic traits and background and teacher’s capabilities. Though perception may
not be related to the students’ performance as revealed after using the PhotoVoice,
the increase in the mean scores of the pretest and posttest could not be neglected.
The result implies that students’ perception may not reflect their post-test scores
due to different factors or variables.

o
Conclusion
Based on the findings, the researchers made the following conclusions:
1. PhotoVoice generally improves the students’ over-all understanding of the
concepts covered, facilitates interaction and communication, and
contributes positively to their learning experiences. Based on the weighted
mean obtained from the three (3) parameters, namely Convenience,
Interest, Ease, and Effectiveness, Measurement and Academic
Achievement, and Communication and Interaction, it respectively
indicates that students strongly agrees that PhotoVoice helped them with
their performance.
20

2. There is an increase in the performance of the students as results showed
that post-test scores were higher than pretest scores based on the mean
obtained from the scores. This just implies that PhotoVoice helped
improve their academic performance.
3. There is a significant difference between the pre-test and post-test scores
of the respondents based on the results obtained from the statistical
analysis. Hence, the intervention used is found out to enhance the students’
academic performance.
4. There is no significant relationship between the students’ posttest scores
and perception. With a p-value of 0.341, the null hypothesis is not rejected.
The result implies that students’ perception may not reflect their post-test
scores due to different factors that contributes to the increase of the mean
scores of the students other than PhotoVoice such as students’ personality
traits and background and teacher’s levels of experience.
Recommendations
Based on the conclusions, the researchers recommends the following:
1. Lengthen the implementation of PhotoVoice as a teaching and learning
tool to observe its effectiveness for a longer period of time.
2. Educators may consider using PhotoVoice in teaching other science
concepts to promote learning through real-life involvement to the students
in order to further enhance their academic performance.
3. The curricular implementors may utilize the results of this study by giving
full identification and understanding of the students’ perception and
learning experiences from PhotoVoice to help them design other
innovative instructions and strategies to further improve teaching-learning
process.
4. Future researchers may consider using experimental research where there
is a control and experimental group to build on this study and further
investigate the effectiveness of using PhotoVoice alone on the target
participants to further enhance the validity and reliability of the study

WHERE ARE THEY NOW? A TRACER STUDY OF SCIENCE
EDUCATION GRADUATES OF GORDON COLLEGE 2017-2022
Kylene Andrea R. Alim 1
Arturo C. Serdena 1
Kristoffer Paul C. Obispo 2
1Researchers: BSEd-Science, Gordon College
2Research Adviser: BSEd-Science, Gordon College
ABSTRACT
This tracer study aimed to determine the employability and graduates' rating
of their academic program from 2017 to 2022. It is a descriptive-quantitative
survey utilizing the modified Commission on Higher Education (CHED)
Graduate Tracer Study Questionnaire. Out of 71 Bachelor of Secondary
Education (BSED) Science graduates from 2017 to 2022, there were only 53
(75%) who participated. The findings revealed that graduates of the BSED
Science program had a good employability rate, and the majority of them have
found employment in fields linked to their undergraduate degrees.
Additionally, the outcomes demonstrated that Gordon College BSED
Science program alumni were in complete agreement with quality teaching,
student activities engagement, learning environment, and student support
services including the BSED Science program outcomes based on the CHED
Memo No. 75 series of 2017, at the said institution. It indicated that indicators
were acknowledged and used within the organization to improve the facilitation
of a comprehensive and concretely responsive learning experience for
graduates. The study suggested that the school consistently provide effective
quality teaching, leadership, academic program dynamic evaluation, and
professional development of the teachers who play a critical role in the
implementation of an effective academic program.
Keywords: Tracer Study, Employability, Ratings, Quality Teaching, Student
Activities Engagement, Learning Environment, Student support services.
INTRODUCTION
Education is an influential step in staying competitive and enhancing
your skills. Whether you’re starting out in your career or returning to continue to
build upon your years of experience, pursuing educational training helps you keep
moving forward (Smith, 2018). Whether they seek undergraduate or graduate
degrees, they are given the knowledge and abilities they need to succeed in the
profession. To comprehend the educational sides of their employment, for
instance, they will learn best practices in teaching in a degree program in
24

education. Pursuing work aligned to their graduate course helps them to be more
productive and flexible to their workplace. Satisfaction, contentment, and
fulfillment are produced when their career and basic values are in sync. To evaluate
the program, employability and competencies acquired in the institution after
graduation, it is important to have tracer study.
Tracer studies are essential information about graduates of academic
programs at Higher Education Institutions (Dela Cruz, 2022). Higher Educational
Institutions (HEI) frequently use tracer studies to get feedback on their programs,
learn how training has helped graduates build their competencies, to monitor and
evaluate the influence of training on graduates’ employability or success. Also,
tracer studies were carried out to see whether the quality assurance of a particular
institution is successful in making its graduates more employable. The
effectiveness can be judged according to the success or failure of the graduates to
make use of their acquired skills and training for economics.
Likewise, a tracer study is a graduate or alumni survey that attempts to
trace the activities of the graduates or previous students of an educational
institution (Millington, n.d.). Tracer study is unquestionably one of the most
important elements to Determine the success of the programs or courses the school
offers. It documents the status, characteristics of employment, struggles, and extent
of applicability of skills in the current employment of the graduates (Castro, 2017).
Tracer studies enable the contextualization of graduates of a particular university
through a system that is dynamic and reliable in order to determine their life path
or movement (The Association of African Universities (AAU), 2002 and Boaduo,
et. al, 2009).
Employability could be defined as graduates demonstrating the attributes
to obtain jobs. It also relates to the employment rates of university graduates
(Sannadan et al., 2016). Trace study is crucial for Higher Education Institutions to
track the success or employment status of the graduates. In terms of graduates’
employability, it may vary in different sectors. The public sector employs workers
through the federal, state or local government and it is tax-payer funded and is
service driven. While the private sector employs workers through individual
business owners, corporations or other non-government agencies.
Numerous tracer studies have been carried out in the Philippines to track
the employability of graduates in diverse sectors. On initiatives for science
education, there are, however, very few tracer studies. Many scholars carried out
tracer studies to assess the acceptability, applicability, and alignment of the
25

graduate degree in science offered by their particular university. The study carried
out by Arbis, et al. (2023) indicated that the graduates of BSEd at their respective
institutions have high employability, and most of them landed in professions
related to their undergraduate studies. Further, the results also showed that the
institutions’ BSED program was very effective in terms of quality teaching,
student activity engagement, learning environment, and student support services.
Similar to this, Canizares (2015) carried out a local study to assess the programs
provided by the University of San Carlos by observing its graduates in the teaching
of science and mathematics. According to him, the majority of those who
completed teacher education passed the Licensure Examination for Teachers
(LET), and the majority of them are currently working as full-time, permanent
teachers.
Employers and students can compare various universities using the
graduates' ratings in order to make educated decisions about where to attend
college and from which college student to hire. Institutions that uphold a high
standard of education and turn out graduates with the necessary skills are attractive
to students and businesses. Additionally, it aids the institution they came from in
raising the standard of instruction.
Gordon College Olongapo City, Bachelor of Secondary Education Major
in Science has a graduate of over 70 students by the year of 2017-2022. From this
number of graduates the college was not able to keep the complete database or
record and whereabouts of the said graduates. As an outcome to this, the college
department wasn’t able to distinguish where the graduates are, what they do and if
their degree helped on their present job. The BSED major in science is a four-year
teacher education program that prepares secondary science teachers. The degree
program includes a significant number of chemistry and physics courses to prepare
graduates to instruct physical sciences courses, also includes biology and
environmental science. In addition to the professional and general education
courses that gave the science instructors the content and pedagogical
understanding they needed to function as professionals and improve students'
academic performance and scientific education in general, Furthermore, graduates
are required to exemplify the ability to contribute to many types of community
development using their talents and abilities, as well as the ability to care for people
and the environment in order to uphold the greater good.
The focus of this study is to trace the BSED Science Education graduates
of Gordon College from the year of 2017 to 2022. The study specifically presented
the solutions to the following questions about the respondents’ demographic
26

profile, educational background, employment status; to determine their length of
time in acquiring a first job, to also evaluate their ratings of the course in terms of
quality teaching, students’ activities engagement, learning environment, student
support services and to evaluate their suggestions or recommendations in terms of
services, facilities, learning environment and others.

This study aims to assess the factors associated with the employability of
the graduates of bsed major in biological science from the year of 2017-2022.
Specifically, it sought to answer the following questions:
1. What is the profile of the respondents in terms of:
1.1 address;

2.2.type of organization;
2.3.present occupation;

2.6.reason for accepting the first job.
3. How do graduates of bsed science rate gordon college in terms of:
3.1.quality teaching;
3.2.student activities engagement;
3.3.learning environment;
3.4.student support service and;
3.5.bsed science program outcomes.

when grouped according to employment profile in terms of:
4.1.employment status;
4.2.type of organization and;
4.3.job relation to program.
